proposals.title: Re-evaluating Combination Chemotherapy Versus Allogeneic Transplant-Based Strategies in Aggressive Adult T-cell Leukemia/Lymphoma: A Retrospective Multi-Centre Outcomes Analysis
proposals.motivation: Adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma (ATLL) remains one of the most treatment-resistant haematological malignancies, with aggressive subtypes achieving median overall survival of only 5.5 months despite initial response rates of approximately 63% to conventional combination chemotherapy regimens such as VCAP-AMP-VECP. This stark disconnect between response and survival suggests that chemotherapy-induced remissions are transient and that current first-line standards may delay but not meaningfully alter disease trajectory. Given emerging evidence that allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (allo-HSCT) can confer durable graft-versus-ATLL effects, a systematic comparison of treatment strategy outcomes is urgently needed to guide clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that patients with aggressive ATLL who proceed to allogeneic transplantation in first remission achieve significantly longer overall survival and progression-free survival compared to those maintained on combination chemotherapy alone, and that early transplant referral reduces the futility of repeated chemotherapy cycles.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multi-centre cohort study across UK NHS transplant centres, leveraging registry data from BSBMT and JACIE-accredited programmes to identify all patients diagnosed with acute or lymphoma-type ATLL between 2010 and 2025. Patients will be stratified into three arms: combination chemotherapy only, chemotherapy followed by allo-HSCT, and chemotherapy followed by alternative consolidation (e.g., mogamulizumab, interferon-alpha/zidovudine). Primary endpoints are overall survival at 12 and 24 months; secondary endpoints include progression-free survival, treatment-related mortality, and graft-versus-host disease incidence. Propensity score matching will adjust for age, performance status, HTLV-1 proviral load, and disease subtype. Cox proportional hazards models will quantify the independent effect of transplant timing on survival.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale UK evidence base directly comparing chemotherapy-only versus transplant-inclusive strategies for aggressive ATLL, informing whether current chemotherapy-centric guidelines should be revised. The findings will support development of risk-stratified treatment algorithms that identify patients most likely to benefit from early allo-HSCT referral, potentially reshaping survivorship care pathways for this rare but devastating disease.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior single-centre Japanese cohorts that dominate ATLL literature, this study applies a multi-centre UK NHS framework with JACIE-accredited transplant outcome data and propensity-matched design, addressing the critical gap of whether chemotherapy as standard first-line treatment represents clinical futility in a non-endemic population setting.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Multicenter Trial of Standardized Opportunistic Infection Prophylaxis Integrated into First-Line Chemotherapy for Adult T-Cell Leukemia/Lymphoma (ATLL)
proposals.motivation: Adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma (ATLL), associated with HTLV-1, carries a dismal prognosis due to both aggressive disease and profound treatment-related immunosuppression. Pre-2026 standard-of-care chemotherapy, such as the LSG15 regimen, induces severe neutropenia and lymphopenia, leading to high rates of opportunistic infections (OIs) like Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and cytomegalovirus reactivation, which are major contributors to early mortality. This problem is exacerbated in the post-allogeneic transplant setting, a curative-intent therapy for eligible patients, where infection risk is already paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a systematic, multi-agent prophylactic protocol against bacterial, fungal, and viral pathogens directly into first-line ATLL chemotherapy will significantly reduce the incidence of severe opportunistic infections and treatment-related mortality, without compromising treatment efficacy or increasing toxicity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multicenter phase II trial enrolling patients with aggressive ATLL scheduled for first-line chemotherapy. The intervention arm will receive a standardized OI prophylaxis bundle (e.g., levofloxacin, posaconazole, valganciclovir, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) initiated concurrently with chemotherapy and continued through the treatment cycles and subsequent neutropenic periods. The primary endpoint will be the incidence of grade ≥3 OIs within the first 120 days. Secondary endpoints include treatment-related mortality, overall survival, and rates of chemotherapy dose modifications. We will employ Cox proportional hazards models and logistic regression to analyze outcomes, adjusting for known risk factors such as age, ATLL subtype, and performance status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective evidence for a standardized, integrated OI prophylaxis strategy in ATLL, directly addressing a major cause of early death. It will generate a practical clinical protocol that can be rapidly adopted to improve patient safety during intensive therapy. Furthermore, by reducing infection-related complications, it may improve the feasibility and outcomes of subsequent consolidative therapies, including allogeneic stem cell transplantation.
proposals.novelty: While OI prophylaxis is routine in other hematologic malignancies and post-transplant care, its systematic integration into ATLL-specific frontline regimens has not been prospectively validated. This proposal uniquely tailors a comprehensive prophylactic strategy to the distinct immunosuppressive profile and infection risks of ATLL, moving beyond ad hoc clinical practice.

proposals.title: Subtype-Stratified Treatment Protocols for Adult T-Cell Leukaemia/Lymphoma: A Multicentre Prospective Trial Comparing Risk-Adapted Therapy Across Shimoyama Subtypes
proposals.motivation: Adult T-cell leukaemia/lymphoma (ATLL) is classified into four clinical subtypes—acute, lymphoma, chronic, and smouldering—with dramatically different natural histories, yet most centres apply uniform intensive chemotherapy regimens. This one-size-fits-all approach exposes indolent-subtype patients to unnecessary toxicity while potentially under-treating aggressive subtypes that might benefit from early allogeneic transplantation. A stratified framework is urgently needed to align treatment intensity with subtype-specific biology and prognosis.
proposals.hypothesis: A subtype-stratified treatment protocol that assigns watchful waiting for smouldering ATLL, interferon-alpha plus zidovudine for chronic unfavourable ATLL, intensive chemotherapy (mLSG15) followed by allogeneic stem cell transplantation for acute ATLL, and CHOP-based chemotherapy with consolidative transplant for lymphoma-type ATLL will improve two-year overall survival by at least 15% compared to historical uniform chemotherapy cohorts, while reducing grade 3–4 adverse events in indolent subtypes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicentre prospective phase II trial enrolling 200 newly diagnosed ATLL patients across HTLV-1-endemic centres in the UK and Japan, stratified by Shimoyama classification at diagnosis. Patients will be assigned to one of four treatment arms: (1) smouldering—active surveillance with quarterly monitoring of proviral load and soluble IL-2 receptor; (2) chronic unfavourable—interferon-alpha/zidovudine combination; (3) lymphoma-type—CHOP-14 for six cycles with reduced-intensity conditioning allogeneic transplant for eligible patients; (4) acute—mLSG15 induction followed by myeloablative allogeneic stem cell transplantation. Primary endpoint is two-year overall survival per arm versus matched historical controls. Secondary endpoints include progression-free survival, treatment-related mortality, and quality-of-life scores using FACT-BMT. Cox proportional hazards models will adjust for age, performance status, and proviral load, with pre-specified subgroup analyses by CCR4 expression status to guide potential mogamulizumab add-on.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective evidence base for subtype-stratified ATLL management, establishing arm-specific survival benchmarks and toxicity profiles. The results will directly inform clinical guidelines by identifying which subtypes benefit from transplantation versus antiviral therapy, and will generate a biomarker-annotated dataset linking proviral load dynamics and CCR4 expression to treatment response across all four subtypes.
proposals.novelty: While retrospective analyses have suggested differential outcomes by ATLL subtype, no prospective trial has simultaneously compared tailored regimens across all four Shimoyama categories within a single protocol. Integrating allogeneic transplant candidacy assessment, antiviral therapy, and active surveillance under one stratified framework—with embedded translational biomarker analysis—represents a non-obvious synthesis of transplant medicine, virology, and risk-adapted oncology that goes beyond existing single-arm subtype studies.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Controlled Phase II Trial of Allogeneic Mesenchymal Stromal Cells for Steroid-Refractory Acute Graft-versus-Host Disease: Validation of a One-Week Immune Biomarker Panel as an Early Surrogate Endpoint for Efficacy
proposals.motivation: Mesenchymal stromal cell (MSC) therapy shows promise for steroid-refractory acute graft-versus-host disease (SR-aGVHD), but clinical trials are hampered by variable efficacy, high cost, and the lag time to traditional 28-day response endpoints. This delays the assessment of MSC potency and the ability to guide subsequent therapy. An early, validated surrogate endpoint is urgently needed to accelerate clinical development and personalize post-transplant care for high-risk patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite biomarker panel measuring platelet recovery, donor-specific immune cell modulation, and inflammatory cytokines at one week post-MSC infusion will serve as a robust early surrogate endpoint, correlating strongly with the standard 28-day complete and partial response rate in SR-aGVHD.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled Phase II trial in 120 patients with SR-aGVHD. Patients will be randomized 2:1 to receive allogeneic, bone marrow-derived MSCs or placebo. The primary endpoint is the correlation between a pre-specified one-week response (defined as improvement in a composite score of platelet count, T-regulatory cell frequency, and IL-10 levels) and the 28-day overall response rate (ORR). Secondary endpoints include non-relapse mortality and overall survival. Statistical analysis will use logistic regression and receiver operating characteristic curves to validate the surrogate relationship.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, biologically grounded early endpoint for MSC trials, potentially reducing trial duration and cost. It will provide critical mechanistic insight into the immediate immunomodulatory effects of MSCs in a clinical setting. The validated endpoint could be adopted for real-time risk assessment, enabling earlier therapeutic interventions for non-responders.
proposals.novelty: While one-week endpoints are used in sepsis, their rigorous validation as a surrogate for longer-term outcomes in the complex immunobiology of aGVHD is novel. This proposal uniquely integrates Richardson's expertise in transplant outcomes, platelet biology, and post-market clinical study with a focused, early biomarker strategy for cellular therapy.

proposals.title: Dose-Escalation Study of Mesenchymal Stromal Cells for Acute Graft-versus-Host Disease: Defining the Optimal Cell Dose for Early Clinical Response
proposals.motivation: Acute graft-versus-host disease (aGvHD) remains a leading cause of morbidity and mortality after allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplantation, with steroid-refractory cases carrying particularly poor prognosis. Mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) have shown immunomodulatory potential in GvHD treatment, yet clinical trials have used widely varying doses (0.4–10 × 10⁶ cells/kg) with inconsistent efficacy, and no rigorous dose-response relationship has been established. Identifying an optimal MSC dose is critical for designing definitive phase III trials and for improving patient outcomes in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: There exists a non-linear dose-response relationship between infused MSC dose and day-7 clinical response rate in steroid-refractory aGvHD, with a plateau effect above which additional cells confer no incremental benefit but increase cost and infusion-related adverse events.
proposals.method: We propose a phase I/II 3+3 dose-escalation trial enrolling adults with grade II–IV steroid-refractory aGvHD across four dose cohorts (1, 3, 5, and 10 × 10⁶ MSCs/kg), with bone marrow-derived, third-party donor MSCs manufactured under GMP conditions. The primary endpoint is overall response rate (complete or partial response) at day 7 post-infusion, assessed using modified IBMTR criteria. Secondary endpoints include day-28 response, overall survival at 100 days, cumulative incidence of chronic GvHD, and biomarker kinetics (serum IL-6, TNF-α, elafin, REG3α) measured at baseline, day 3, 7, and 28. A Bayesian optimal interval (BOIN) design will guide dose escalation to identify the optimal biological dose maximizing efficacy while monitoring for dose-limiting toxicities including infusion reactions and cytopenias. Correlative immune profiling via flow cytometry will assess T-regulatory cell expansion and Th1/Th17 suppression at each dose level.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic dose-response curve for MSC therapy in aGvHD, establishing an evidence-based optimal dose for subsequent pivotal trials. The integrated biomarker analysis will identify pharmacodynamic markers predictive of early response, enabling future patient stratification. These findings will directly inform clinical guidelines for MSC dosing in the post-transplant setting.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior MSC trials that selected doses empirically or tested single fixed doses, this study applies a formal dose-escalation framework with Bayesian adaptive design specifically powered to detect a dose-response plateau, coupled with mechanistic biomarker profiling to link cell dose to immunological effect—an approach not previously applied to MSC therapy in GvHD.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omic Analysis of Pre-Infusion Plasma to Predict Early Clinical Response to Mesenchymal Stromal Cell Therapy for Steroid-Refractory Acute Graft-Versus-Host Disease
proposals.motivation: Mesenchymal stromal cell (MSC) infusions are a promising therapy for steroid-refractory acute Graft-Versus-Host Disease (GvHD), but clinical responses are heterogeneous and unpredictable. Identifying patients likely to respond is a critical unmet need to optimize treatment allocation, improve patient outcomes, and reduce the risk of ineffective, costly interventions. This aligns with Richardson's focus on transplant patient outcomes and risk stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pre-infusion signature of circulating proteins, metabolites, and immune cell-derived extracellular vesicles in patient plasma will distinguish future responders from non-responders to MSC therapy within one week of treatment.
proposals.method: We will perform a prospective, observational study in allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplant patients receiving MSCs for steroid-refractory acute GvHD. Pre-infusion plasma samples will be analyzed using: 1) Olink® targeted proteomics (inflammation and oncology panels), 2) untargeted liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) metabolomics, and 3) nanoparticle tracking analysis and flow cytometry of platelet and immune cell-derived extracellular vesicles. A Random Forest classifier, trained on 70% of the cohort with 10-fold cross-validation, will integrate these multi-omic features to predict binary clinical response at day 7, with performance validated on the held-out 30%.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, clinically actionable biomarker panel for early prediction of MSC efficacy in GvHD. It will advance the field by moving beyond post-hoc analysis of MSC mechanisms to a pre-treatment decision tool. The findings will directly inform the design of future stratified clinical trials and personalize care for high-risk transplant patients.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies examine MSC mechanisms in vitro or post-infusion immune changes, this proposal uniquely focuses on a pre-treatment, multi-omic predictive signature for a rapid (one-week) clinical endpoint, integrating extracellular vesicle profiles not previously assessed in this context.

proposals.title: Deciphering the Organ-Specific Stromal and Immune Microenvironment Underlying Differential Mesenchymal Stromal Cell Efficacy in Graft-versus-Host Disease
proposals.motivation: Mesenchymal stromal cell (MSC) therapy for steroid-refractory acute graft-versus-host disease (GvHD) shows variable clinical efficacy, with response patterns often differing by involved organ (e.g., gut vs. skin). This heterogeneity limits treatment predictability and patient benefit. Understanding the organ-specific biological determinants of MSC response is critical to optimize this promising cell-based therapy for allogeneic transplant patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the functional efficacy of administered MSCs is determined by pre-existing, organ-specific differences in the host stromal niche and local immune milieu, which either support or inhibit MSC immunomodulatory activity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective clinical study of allogeneic transplant patients with steroid-refractory GvHD receiving standard-of-care MSC therapy. Pre- and post-infusion biopsies from affected organs (skin, gastrointestinal tract) will be analyzed using spatial transcriptomics (Visium) and multiplex immunofluorescence to map local stromal cell states, immune cell subsets, and chemokine landscapes. These high-dimensional data will be integrated with clinical response outcomes using machine learning (Random Forest and logistic regression models) to identify organ-specific microenvironmental signatures predictive of MSC therapeutic success.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the first organ-specific molecular and cellular signatures predictive of MSC therapy response in GvHD. It will move the field beyond clinical staging towards a mechanistic, biomarker-driven understanding of treatment heterogeneity. The findings will directly inform the development of rational, organ-targeted therapeutic strategies, such as preconditioning regimens or combination therapies, to enhance MSC efficacy.
proposals.novelty: While MSCs are known to have heterogeneous effects, prior studies have not systematically compared the pathological microenvironment across different involved organs within the same patient cohort using spatial multi-omics to explain this variation. Our approach uniquely links detailed organ-specific host biology directly to clinical outcomes in a real-world treatment setting.

proposals.title: A Bayesian adaptive platform trial of post-transplant cyclophosphamide regimens using early platelet recovery to guide therapy escalation in high-risk allogeneic stem cell transplant patients
proposals.motivation: Patients undergoing allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant for high-risk diseases remain at significant risk of graft failure and poor engraftment, leading to increased mortality. Current prophylactic regimens, like post-transplant cyclophosphamide (PTCy), are applied uniformly, but a subset of patients show early signs of poor graft function, such as delayed platelet recovery, which is a critical prognostic indicator. An adaptive strategy to identify and escalate therapy for these non-responders in real-time could improve clinical outcomes and personalize transplant medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: That early platelet count recovery at one week post-transplant can serve as a robust biomarker to identify patients at high risk for poor engraftment, and that an adaptive intervention adding a second agent (e.g., a thrombopoietin receptor agonist) for these non-responders will improve the rate and durability of platelet engraftment compared to a standard, non-adaptive PTCy regimen.
proposals.method: We propose a Bayesian response-adaptive platform trial. Patients receive standard PTCy-based graft-versus-host disease prophylaxis. The primary adaptive endpoint is platelet count at day +7. A Bayesian logistic model will continuously evaluate the probability of a patient being a 'non-responder' based on this count and pre-specified covariates. Patients whose probability exceeds a pre-defined threshold will be randomized within the trial to either continue standard care or to receive an escalated therapy arm (e.g., adding eltrombopag). The adaptation algorithm will use Thompson sampling to dynamically allocate patients to the more promising arm based on accumulating data for the secondary endpoint of platelet engraftment by day +30.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clinical framework for real-time, biomarker-driven personalization of transplant supportive care, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all approach. It will generate high-quality evidence on the efficacy of early therapeutic escalation based on platelet recovery and establish a methodological blueprint for adaptive trials in the complex, time-sensitive transplant setting.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive designs are used in oncology, their application to guide *supportive care escalation* within days of transplant based on a simple, routine lab value is novel. This integrates real-time clinical response into a dynamic treatment algorithm specifically for transplant patients, a population where early intervention is critical but understudied in adaptive trials.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Profiling of Immune Reconstitution and Graft-Versus-Host Disease Biomarkers in Allogeneic Transplant Recipients Treated with Mesenchymal Stromal Cells
proposals.motivation: Mesenchymal stromal cell (MSC) infusions are used to treat steroid-refractory acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) after allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT), but clinical responses are highly variable. Pre-2026, the precise immunological mechanisms driving response or resistance remain poorly characterized, limiting patient selection and therapeutic optimization. This gap is critical in the context of post-transplant survivorship, where effective aGVHD management directly impacts long-term outcomes and quality of life.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that clinical response to MSC therapy is driven by specific, measurable shifts in the composition, transcriptional state, and cell-cell communication networks of recipient immune cells, particularly in T cell and monocyte subsets, which can be identified by single-cell profiling.
proposals.method: We will perform single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) using the 10x Genomics Chromium platform on serial peripheral blood mononuclear cell (PBMC) samples from allogeneic HCT patients with steroid-refractory aGVHD, collected pre- and post-MSC infusion. Cell Ranger will be used for initial processing and alignment. Downstream analysis will employ Seurat for clustering, differential expression, and trajectory inference, and CellPhoneDB to analyze ligand-receptor interactions. Patients will be stratified into clinical responders and non-responders based on standard aGVHD grading criteria.
proposals.contribution: This study will define the immune cell atlas associated with MSC response, identifying predictive cellular and transcriptional biomarkers for patient stratification. It will elucidate mechanistic pathways of MSC immunomodulation in a clinical transplant setting, informing future combination therapies. The findings will directly contribute to personalized, risk-adapted management of aGVHD, a major cause of post-transplant morbidity.
proposals.novelty: While single-cell studies of MSC mechanisms exist in vitro or in murine models, applying this high-resolution technology directly to serial samples from a defined, high-risk clinical cohort of allogeneic transplant patients represents a significant translational advance. The integration of deep immunophenotyping with rigorous clinical response data will move beyond correlative observations to reveal causal pathways of treatment success or failure.

proposals.title: Prophylactic Mesenchymal Stromal Cell Infusion to Modulate Early Immune Reconstitution and Prevent Chronic Graft-versus-Host Disease Following Allogeneic Hematopoietic Cell Transplant: A Phase II Randomized Trial
proposals.motivation: Chronic graft-versus-host disease (cGvHD) remains a leading cause of late morbidity and mortality after allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT), severely impacting long-term patient survivorship and quality of life. Current mesenchymal stromal cell (MSC) therapy is used reactively for steroid-refractory disease, with variable efficacy. Shifting this paradigm from treatment to prevention could fundamentally improve long-term outcomes for transplant recipients, a population of growing importance given expanding transplant indications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that prophylactic infusion of third-party, bone marrow-derived MSCs at the time of transplant will modulate early immune reconstitution, promoting regulatory over inflammatory pathways, and thereby significantly reduce the incidence and severity of cGvHD without compromising graft-versus-leukemia effects.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, randomized, open-label Phase II trial in adult patients undergoing matched unrelated donor HCT for acute leukemia or myelodysplastic syndrome. Patients will be randomized to receive standard prophylaxis alone or with two intravenous doses of cryopreserved, off-the-shelf MSCs (2x10^6 cells/kg) on day 0 and day +7 post-transplant. The primary endpoint is the cumulative incidence of moderate-severe cGvHD at one year using NIH consensus criteria. Secondary endpoints include overall survival, relapse, and immune reconstitution profiling via high-dimensional flow cytometry (e.g., CyTOF) and multiplex cytokine analysis at serial timepoints (days +30, +100, +180) to identify predictive immune signatures.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level clinical evidence for the prophylactic use of MSCs in HCT, potentially establishing a new standard of care for cGvHD prevention. It will also deliver a comprehensive longitudinal dataset on early post-transplant immune cell subsets and soluble mediators associated with cGvHD development, offering mechanistic insights and biomarkers for future risk stratification.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the established clinical application of MSCs from salvage therapy for established cGvHD to upfront prophylaxis, a conceptually distinct strategy. It integrates detailed clinical outcomes with deep immune phenotyping to move beyond correlation towards understanding the mechanistic impact of MSCs on the evolving post-transplant immune landscape.

proposals.title: Defining Age-Specific Dosing and Immunomodulatory Response Kinetics of Allogeneic Mesenchymal Stromal Cells for Pediatric Steroid-Refractory Acute Graft-versus-Host Disease
proposals.motivation: Mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) are a promising therapy for steroid-refractory acute Graft-versus-Host Disease (SR-aGvHD) following allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplant. However, dosing regimens are empirically derived from adult studies, ignoring critical pediatric-specific factors like body composition, immune system maturation, and disease kinetics. This gap risks under-dosing or toxicity in children and hampers the development of standardized, effective protocols for this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an MSC dosing strategy normalized to pediatric body surface area and lymphocyte count will demonstrate superior and more predictable immunomodulatory efficacy and clinical response kinetics in SR-aGvHD compared to standard weight-based dosing.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-center phase II study in pediatric SR-aGvHD patients, randomizing to standard weight-based (e.g., 2x10^6 cells/kg) versus a novel BSA/lymphocyte-adjusted dosing arm. Primary endpoint is Day 28 overall response. Secondary endpoints include kinetics of response via daily clinical scoring and serial immune profiling (multiplex cytokine analysis, T-regulatory cell quantification by flow cytometry, and donor-specific antibody monitoring). Pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic modeling will be performed using non-linear mixed-effects models (NONMEM) to correlate MSC exposure (via donor-derived DNA in plasma) with immune biomarker trajectories and clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence-based, pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic rationale for pediatric-specific MSC dosing in SR-aGvHD. It will deliver a validated dosing algorithm and define age-specific response biomarkers, directly informing clinical trial design and personalized treatment protocols to improve outcomes for pediatric transplant patients.
proposals.novelty: While MSC use in GvHD is established, this study moves beyond simple weight-based scaling to develop a mechanistic, physiology-informed dosing model for children, integrating real-time immune profiling with exposure data—an approach not yet applied in pediatric cellular therapy.

proposals.title: Prevalence and Clinical Impact of Occult Germline Predisposition in Adults with Apparent De Novo Acute Myeloid Leukemia: A Prospective Multicenter Study
proposals.motivation: Current clinical practice often assumes adult AML is sporadic, limiting routine germline testing. However, emerging pre-2026 evidence suggests inherited syndromes like Fanconi anemia can present with adult-onset AML. This oversight risks catastrophic toxicity from standard DNA-damaging transplant conditioning and inappropriate donor selection from affected relatives, directly impacting patient safety and transplant outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: A clinically significant subset of adults diagnosed with apparent de novo AML harbors occult, pathogenic germline variants in genes associated with inherited bone marrow failure and myeloid malignancy syndromes, which reclassifies their disease ontology and necessitates altered clinical management.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study across 5-10 UK transplant centers, enrolling 500 consecutive adults (≥18y) with newly diagnosed AML. All patients will undergo germline DNA analysis via a custom, clinically validated next-generation sequencing panel covering >50 genes (e.g., DDX41, RUNX1, GATA2, FA pathway genes, telomere biology disorders). Variant calling will use GATK best practices, and classification will follow ACMG/AMP 2015 guidelines. Clinical data on treatment response, transplant conditioning, donor selection, and outcomes will be correlated with germline status.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first robust, prospective estimate of occult germline predisposition in adult AML, directly informing revisions to diagnostic guidelines. It will establish a clinical framework for integrating germline results into risk stratification, conditioning regimen choice, and allogeneic donor selection to improve patient safety and family counseling.
proposals.novelty: This systematically challenges the age-based diagnostic paradigm by applying comprehensive germline screening universally in adults, moving beyond targeted testing based on family history or young age, which is the pre-2026 standard.

proposals.title: A Phase I/II Study of a Response-Adapted, DNA-Repair-Aware Chemotherapy Platform for Acute Myeloid Leukemia in Patients with Fanconi Anemia
proposals.motivation: Fanconi Anemia (FA) patients who develop AML have a dismal prognosis due to extreme sensitivity to DNA-damaging chemotherapy, leading to unacceptable toxicity with standard regimens. This mirrors the historical challenge in HIV-associated lymphomas, where immunocompromise necessitated adapted protocols. No standardized, biologically rational treatment strategy exists for FA-AML, creating an urgent unmet clinical need.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the dose-adjusted, response-guided paradigm from HIV-oncology (e.g., DA-EPOCH-R) to FA-AML, using DNA-repair biomarkers for real-time dose titration and integrating targeted agents, will improve the therapeutic index and clinical outcomes compared to historical approaches.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-arm, multicenter Phase I/II trial. The backbone is CPX-351 initiated at 50% standard dose, with cycle-by-cycle dose adjustments (20% increments/decrements) guided by time to neutrophil recovery and DEB-induced chromosomal breakage rates as a pharmacodynamic biomarker of DNA repair stress. All patients receive venetoclax, with FLT3+ patients receiving gilteritinib. Response is assessed via flow cytometry and next-generation sequencing for minimal residual disease (MRD). The primary endpoint is the rate of composite complete remission (CR/CRi) with ≤Grade 4 non-hematologic toxicity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first prospective, biomarker-driven treatment protocol for FA-AML, directly addressing the critical toxicity-efficacy balance. It will generate essential data on the safety and activity of CPX-351 and venetoclax in this DNA-repair deficient population and validate DEB-induced breakage as a dynamic, clinically actionable biomarker for chemotherapy dosing.
proposals.novelty: The proposal innovatively translates a response-adapted dosing framework from HIV-lymphoma to a congenital DNA repair disorder context, a cross-disciplinary analogy not previously applied. It integrates real-time DNA damage biomarker feedback with modern targeted agents, moving beyond empiric dose reduction.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Immunophenotypic Profiling of CD66 Variants to Guide Adaptive Chemotherapy for Acute Myeloid Leukemia in Fanconi Anemia Patients
proposals.motivation: Patients with Fanconi anemia (FA) who develop acute myeloid leukemia (AML) face a dire therapeutic impasse: their profound DNA repair defect precludes standard-intensity induction chemotherapy, yet reduced-intensity regimens are often empiric and ineffective. There is a critical unmet need for biomarkers that can stratify leukemic subclones by chemosensitivity within the narrow therapeutic window of FA. Pre-2026 evidence implicates CD66 (CEACAM) family variants in cell adhesion, signaling, and drug response in myeloid malignancies, but their role in FA-AML heterogeneity is unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: High-dimensional single-cell profiling of CD66 variant expression on FA-AML blasts will reveal distinct immunophenotypic signatures that correlate with in vitro chemosensitivity, enabling the rational selection of reduced-intensity chemotherapy regimens tailored to target the most vulnerable leukemic subpopulations.
proposals.method: We will apply mass cytometry (CyTOF) to primary AML blasts from FA patients (n=20-25) at diagnosis, using a custom antibody panel targeting CD66 isoforms (CEACAM1, 5, 6, 8), lineage markers, and functional proteins (e.g., BCL-2, MDR1). Single-cell data will be analyzed using established pre-2026 computational pipelines (e.g., viSNE, PhenoGraph) to identify blast subpopulations. These immunophenotypic clusters will be correlated with ex vivo drug sensitivity testing (DST) to a panel of reduced-intensity agents (e.g., low-dose cytarabine, fludarabine, venetoclax). A predictive model will be built using regularized regression (LASSO) to link CD66 signatures to DST outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish CD66 variant immunophenotyping as a novel functional biomarker layer for FA-AML, moving clinical decision-making beyond generic dose reduction. It will provide the first high-resolution map of chemosensitivity-linked clonal architecture in FA-AML, directly informing the design of adaptive, patient-specific combination regimens. The study will generate a translatable CyTOF-based framework for precision oncology in this ultra-high-risk population.
proposals.novelty: While CyTOF has been used in general AML profiling, its application to FA-AML for chemotherapy personalization is unprecedented. The fusion of single-cell CD66 variant phenotyping with functional drug testing creates a non-obvious, mechanism-informed strategy to navigate the unique therapeutic constraints of FA, diverging from prior generic pharmacodynamic studies.

proposals.title: A Phase II Study of Reduced-Intensity Fludarabine-Cytarabine with Venetoclax for Acute Myeloid Leukaemia Arising in Fanconi Anaemia: A Risk-Adapted Protocol
proposals.motivation: Fanconi anaemia (FA) patients who develop acute myeloid leukaemia (AML) face exceptionally poor outcomes because standard intensive chemotherapy causes life-threatening toxicity due to their underlying DNA repair deficiency. Current management relies on ad hoc dose reductions without prospective evidence, leading to unacceptable treatment-related mortality exceeding 40% in historical cohorts. There is an urgent unmet need for a rationally designed, evidence-based chemotherapy protocol that balances disease control with tolerability in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: A risk-adapted regimen combining reduced-intensity fludarabine-cytarabine (30% dose reduction from standard AML induction) with the BCL-2 inhibitor venetoclax will achieve comparable complete remission rates to standard AML therapy (target ≥55%) while reducing treatment-related mortality to below 15% in FA patients with AML.
proposals.method: This multicentre phase II trial will enrol FA patients aged 2-40 years with newly diagnosed AML across UK and European transplant centres. The induction protocol consists of fludarabine (20 mg/m² days 1-5) and cytarabine (1 g/m² days 1-5) at approximately 70% of standard dosing, combined with venetoclax (ramp-up to 400 mg daily, days 1-28), exploiting the selective BCL-2 dependency observed in FA-associated myeloid clones. Pharmacokinetic sampling at days 1, 3, and 5 will guide individualised dose adjustments using a Bayesian adaptive model incorporating patient weight, renal function, and chromosomal breakage assay severity. Responders will be bridged to reduced-intensity conditioning allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplantation using fludarabine-cyclophosphamide protocols established in FA transplant practice. Primary endpoints are complete remission rate and day-100 treatment-related mortality; secondary endpoints include measurable residual disease clearance, event-free survival at 2 years, and pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic correlations.
proposals.contribution: This study would establish the first prospectively validated chemotherapy protocol specifically designed for FA-AML, providing an evidence base to replace current empirical dose modifications. The pharmacokinetic modelling framework could be extended to other DNA repair-deficient populations, and the integration of venetoclax into reduced-intensity backbones may define a new standard bridging strategy to transplant for FA patients.
proposals.novelty: While venetoclax combinations have transformed AML treatment in elderly and unfit patients, no study has systematically exploited the heightened BCL-2 dependency of FA-derived myeloid clones or used Bayesian pharmacokinetic adaptation calibrated to chromosomal fragility as a dosing biomarker, representing a precision approach uniquely tailored to FA biology.

proposals.title: A FA-First Ex Vivo Synthetic Lethality Screen to Derive a Patient-Specific Chemotherapy Guide for Allogeneic Transplant Conditioning in Fanconi Anemia
proposals.motivation: Patients with Fanconi Anemia (FA) have defective DNA repair and extreme sensitivity to standard alkylator-based conditioning regimens for allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplant (HCT), leading to high treatment-related mortality. Pre-2026, pharmacogenomic data from non-FA oncology is used to infer FA drug responses, but this is risky as FA cells have a unique vulnerability landscape. A direct, systematic assessment of chemotherapy toxicity in primary FA patient cells is urgently needed to develop safer, personalized transplant protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a primary FA cell-centric drug screen will identify agent-specific synthetic lethal interactions and define a safe therapeutic index for conditioning drugs, which can then be refined with constrained pharmacogenomic modeling to create a robust, patient-specific dosing guide.
proposals.method: We will establish a high-throughput ex vivo drug sensitivity platform using primary bone marrow or peripheral blood mononuclear cells from FA patients and isogenic corrected controls. Cells will be screened against a library of 150+ agents, including alkylators, platinum drugs, and targeted therapies. Dose-response curves will be analyzed using the BREEZE algorithm to calculate FA-specific lethal concentration (LC50) and therapeutic index. Agents with a favorable index will be modeled using the PharmacoGx package in R, integrating limited, relevant oncology pharmacogenomic data (e.g., from TP53-mutant cancers) to predict inter-patient variability and resistance, strictly bounded by the FA-derived safety window.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first FA-centric chemosensitivity atlas, directly linking specific FA gene defects to drug toxicity profiles. It will deliver a clinically actionable framework for selecting and dosing conditioning agents for individual FA patients undergoing HCT, potentially reducing transplant-related morbidity. The 'FA-first' methodology establishes a new paradigm for therapy development in ultra-rare, genomically unstable diseases.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel because it inverts the standard paradigm by defining the FA-specific toxicity landscape *de novo* before applying any external pharmacogenomic data, making it inherently safer than extrapolation from general oncology. It integrates high-throughput primary cell screening with constrained computational modeling specifically for transplant conditioning personalization.

proposals.title: Pharmacological Priming with an Oral Isoxsuprine Formulation to Enhance Chromosomal Breakage Assay Sensitivity for Detecting Cryptic Genomic Instability in Young Adult AML
proposals.motivation: A significant subset of young adult AML patients, particularly those with secondary disease or cryptic Fanconi Anemia (FA) pathway deficiencies, exhibit genomic instability that is not reliably detected by standard chromosomal breakage assays like the diepoxybutane (DEB) test. This diagnostic gap can delay identification of high-risk disease and associated therapeutic vulnerabilities, impacting transplant and post-transplant care strategies. Enhancing the sensitivity of this assay is critical for timely risk stratification and personalized treatment planning.
proposals.hypothesis: Pre-treatment with a safe, orally bioavailable agent that induces mild DNA replication stress will selectively exacerbate chromosomal fragility in latent FA-deficient or genomically unstable AML blasts, thereby amplifying their breakage response to a subsequent DEB challenge and increasing the diagnostic yield of the chromosomal breakage assay.
proposals.method: We will develop a modified-release oral formulation of isoxsuprine HCl, a known beta-adrenergic agonist with mild DNA fragility-inducing properties, to ensure sustained systemic exposure. In a phase I/II clinical study, young adult AML patients will receive a single dose prior to routine diagnostic bone marrow aspiration. Isolated mononuclear cells will undergo the standard DEB-induced chromosomal breakage assay, with breakage frequency quantified by metaphase spread analysis and compared to matched, unprimed samples. Flow cytometry will be used to correlate breakage with cell lineage (CD34+/CD33+ blasts).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel paradigm of pharmacologically enhanced diagnostics for AML. It aims to provide a clinically feasible method to uncover cryptic genomic instability, informing risk-adapted therapy including allogeneic stem cell transplant decisions. The research could also identify a patient subset with 'pharmacologically induced' vulnerabilities, opening avenues for targeted sensitization strategies.
proposals.novelty: This approach repurposes a known safe drug not as a therapy, but as a diagnostic sensitizer, transforming a passive cytogenetic test into an active, pharmacologically primed procedure. The use of a controlled-release oral formulation for this specific diagnostic purpose is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Clinical-Genomic Pre-Screening Model to Guide Fanconi Anemia Pathway Testing in Adult Acute Myeloid Leukemia
proposals.motivation: Identifying underlying Fanconi Anemia (FA) in adult AML patients is critical for guiding therapy selection, particularly avoiding DNA-damaging agents like alkylators, and informing allogeneic stem cell transplant donor choice. However, universal screening with chromosomal breakage assays or germline sequencing is costly and logistically challenging in adult oncology. A targeted approach is needed to optimize resource use and integrate rare germline predisposition testing into standard workflows for adult patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pre-screening model integrating readily available clinical features and tumor genomic signatures can accurately identify a high-risk subgroup of adult AML patients in whom definitive FA diagnostic testing will have a high positive predictive value, thereby enabling a cost-effective, precision-guided screening strategy.
proposals.method: We will perform a retrospective cohort study of adult AML patients (≥18 years) with available germline FA testing results. Candidate predictors will include: clinical features (personal/family cancer history, physical stigmata, prior chemotherapy/radiation exposure) and tumor genomic data (somatic mutation patterns from targeted NGS panels, focusing on signatures associated with FA-deficiency, such as specific structural variant profiles). A multivariable logistic regression model will be developed to predict a positive germline FA test. Model performance (discrimination, calibration) will be validated in an independent cohort. The final model will define a high-risk threshold for proceeding to confirmatory testing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, practical framework for risk-stratified FA pathway screening in adult AML, directly addressing a gap in survivorship and transplant planning. It will establish a set of high-yield clinical and genomic pre-filters to increase screening efficiency and positive predictive value, facilitating the integration of germline genetics into adult oncology practice.
proposals.novelty: While FA screening is standard in pediatric AML, this approach is novel for the adult setting and moves beyond single-feature triggers (like age) by integrating multidimensional clinical and somatic tumor genomic data into a unified risk-stratification tool.

proposals.title: A Phase I/II Trial of Anti-CEACAM1 Radioimmunotherapy for High-Risk Acute Myeloid Leukemia with Molecularly Defined FA/BRCA Pathway Deficiencies
proposals.motivation: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) patients with DNA repair deficiencies, particularly in the Fanconi Anemia/BRCA (FA/BRCA) pathway, represent a high-risk subset with poor responses to standard chemotherapy and allogeneic stem cell transplant. Pre-2026, targeted therapies for these specific molecular vulnerabilities are lacking. Radioimmunotherapy (RIT) targeting CD66a (CEACAM1), expressed on myeloid cells, offers a potent cytotoxic modality, but its efficacy has not been stratified by underlying DNA repair genotypes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AML blasts with molecularly confirmed defects in the FA/BRCA DNA repair pathway will demonstrate exquisite sensitivity to the DNA double-strand breaks induced by anti-CEACAM1 radioimmunotherapy, leading to superior tumor cell kill and improved clinical responses compared to AML with intact DNA repair.
proposals.method: We will first perform comprehensive genomic and functional screening (using targeted NGS panels for FA/BRCA genes and a functional RAD51 foci formation assay) on primary AML samples to identify FA/BRCA-deficient cases. Eligible patients will receive a standard myeloablative conditioning regimen of fludarabine and total body irradiation, augmented with a single dose of 90Y-labeled anti-CD66a (CEACAM1) monoclonal antibody prior to allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation. The primary endpoint will be safety (Phase I), followed by assessment of minimal residual disease-negative complete remission at day +100 post-transplant (Phase II). Correlative studies will measure DNA damage markers (γ-H2AX, 53BP1) in leukemic blasts pre- and post-RIT.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first genotype-directed clinical trial of radioimmunotherapy in AML, directly linking a specific molecular defect to a targeted cytotoxic mechanism. It will generate crucial safety and efficacy data for CEACAM1-RIT in a high-risk population and provide a mechanistic framework for expanding synthetic lethal approaches to other DNA repair-deficient malignancies.
proposals.novelty: While FA/BRCA deficiency is a known synthetic lethal target for PARP inhibitors in solid tumors, its application to radioimmunotherapy in AML is unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates detailed pre-therapy pathway screening with a clinically established, but genomically unselected, RIT platform to create a novel precision therapy.

proposals.title: A Phase I/II Study of an Optimized Oral Lipid-Based Isoxsuprine Formulation for Chemoprevention of MDS/AML in Adults with Molecular Fanconi Anemia
proposals.motivation: Adults with molecular Fanconi anemia (FA) without classic congenital features represent a growing, high-risk population with extreme predisposition to myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) and acute myeloid leukemia (AML) due to underlying genomic instability. Current management is limited to high-risk allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation or intensive chemotherapy, which are poorly tolerated and carry excessive toxicity in this DNA repair-deficient context. Repurposing isoxsuprine, a vasodilator with preclinical evidence for FA pathway modulation, offers a potential oral chemopreventive strategy, but its clinical utility is severely hampered by historically poor and variable oral bioavailability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reformulating isoxsuprine into an advanced oral lipid-based drug delivery system (LBDDS) will significantly enhance its bioavailability and pharmacokinetic profile, enabling effective and sustained plasma concentrations for FA pathway modulation to delay or prevent MDS/AML onset in adult FA patients.
proposals.method: We will employ a rational formulation design, first screening isoxsuprine solubility in various lipid excipients (e.g., medium-chain triglycerides, Labrasol) and surfactants to develop self-emulsifying drug delivery systems (SEDDS). Lead SEDDS prototypes will be characterized for droplet size, stability, and in vitro drug release. Pharmacokinetic optimization will be conducted in a rodent model, comparing the optimized SEDDS to conventional oral isoxsuprine, with analysis of Cmax, Tmax, and AUC. The lead formulation will then be evaluated in a pilot clinical cohort of adult FA patients (without active MDS/AML) in a Phase I/II trial, assessing safety, tolerability, and pharmacodynamic biomarkers of FA pathway activity (e.g., FANCD2 monoubiquitination in peripheral blood mononuclear cells).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a clinically viable, patient-compliant oral formulation for a repurposed agent specifically targeting FA-associated leukemogenesis. It will establish preliminary safety and pharmacodynamic efficacy data for isoxsuprine in FA patients, creating a foundation for larger chemoprevention trials. The project directly addresses a critical survivorship gap for adult FA patients post-diagnosis but pre-malignancy, where no proactive medical management currently exists.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely merges advanced pharmaceutical formulation science with the niche clinical challenge of adult-onset FA, moving beyond the traditional focus on pediatric FA and bone marrow transplant. It applies a targeted drug delivery strategy not previously attempted for FA pathway modulators to solve a specific pharmacokinetic barrier, enabling a novel chemopreventive clinical paradigm.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Relative Contributions of Humoral and Cellular Immunity to SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine Efficacy in Allogeneic Hematopoietic Cell Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: For immunocompromised patients, such as allogeneic transplant recipients, defining the true correlates of protection from COVID-19 is critical for risk stratification and guiding booster strategies. Pre-2026, anti-spike IgG titers are the primary surrogate endpoint for vaccine efficacy, but emerging data suggest T cell responses may be more durable and critical in preventing severe disease, especially in B-cell depleted or hypogammaglobulinemic patients. This gap is particularly relevant for Susan D. Richardson's focus on transplant patient outcomes and post-vaccine immune responses.
proposals.hypothesis: In allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplant (allo-HCT) recipients, robust SARS-CoV-2-specific T cell responses, measured by IFN-γ ELISpot and activation-induced marker (AIM) assays, will be a stronger correlate of protection against breakthrough SARS-CoV-2 infection and severe COVID-19 than anti-spike antibody titers alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, longitudinal cohort study of 150 allo-HCT patients vaccinated against SARS-CoV-2. Serial blood samples will be collected post-vaccination to quantify anti-spike IgG and neutralizing antibodies. Concurrently, we will measure SARS-CoV-2-specific T cell responses using peptide pool-stimulated IFN-γ ELISpot (for magnitude) and high-dimensional flow cytometry for AIM (CD69+CD137+) on CD4+ and CD8+ T cells (for phenotype and polyfunctionality). Clinical outcomes (PCR-confirmed infection, severity per WHO scale) will be tracked for 12 months. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards models, adjusting for transplant-related risk factors, to determine the independent association of each immune parameter with protection.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a refined, immune-based risk stratification model for allo-HCT patients, moving beyond antibody-centric measures. It will directly inform clinical guidelines on monitoring and supplemental vaccination strategies for this vulnerable population. The findings will challenge the regulatory paradigm of using antibody titers as the primary surrogate endpoint for vaccine efficacy in immunocompromised hosts.
proposals.novelty: While previous work by Richardson and others has described immune responses post-vaccination, this proposal directly tests the comparative predictive value of humoral versus cellular immunity for clinical outcomes in a high-risk, real-world transplant cohort. It integrates detailed T cell phenotyping with rigorous clinical follow-up to establish a causal link between specific immune compartments and protection.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omic Stratification of Immunosuppressed Patients to Predict Vaccine Response and Clinical Risk
proposals.motivation: Immunosuppressed patients, including transplant recipients and those with hematologic malignancies, are managed as a monolithic high-risk group despite profound heterogeneity in vaccine response and infection outcomes. Pre-2026, studies like those on COVID-19 vaccines in immune-suppressive disease show failure rates ranging from 12% to 72%, indicating a critical need for precision stratification. This one-size-fits-all approach hampers clinical trial design, resource allocation, and personalized prophylactic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating deep immunophenotyping with clinical and cellular data will identify distinct, biologically defined endotypes within the broadly 'immunosuppressed' population, which correlate predictably with vaccine immunogenicity and infection risk.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (allo-HSCT) patients and patients with B-cell malignancies. Using pre- and post-vaccination samples, we will perform high-dimensional flow cytometry (40+ parameters), single-cell RNA sequencing of peripheral blood mononuclear cells, and quantify antigen-specific T-cell and B-cell responses. Clinical data, including transplant characteristics, graft-versus-host disease, and prior therapies, will be integrated. We will apply unsupervised machine learning (consensus clustering, Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) to identify patient clusters, followed by supervised learning (random forest regression) to build a predictive model for vaccine failure.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond condition-based risk grouping by providing a data-driven, biologically grounded taxonomy of immunosuppression. It will deliver a validated stratification tool to identify patients most likely to benefit from enhanced vaccine regimens or prophylactic interventions, directly informing post-transplant clinical care guidelines and trial design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work describes response heterogeneity, this study is novel in its application of multi-omic integration and unbiased computational clustering specifically to deconstruct the 'immunosuppressed' umbrella into mechanistic endotypes, linking cellular phenotypes directly to clinical vaccine failure risk.

proposals.title: Beyond Two Doses: A Prospective Study of Alternative Immunization Strategies for B Cell-Depleted Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: Patients with B cell depletion following allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation or anti-CD20 therapy mount severely impaired humoral responses to standard two-dose vaccination regimens, as demonstrated during the COVID-19 pandemic. This leaves a highly vulnerable immunosuppressed population without reliable protective immunity despite guideline-adherent vaccination. Understanding whether two doses are fundamentally insufficient—and identifying superior alternative strategies—is critical for reducing infection-related morbidity and mortality in this growing patient group.
proposals.hypothesis: Standard two-dose vaccination regimens are fundamentally insufficient to generate durable protective humoral and cellular immunity in B cell-depleted patients, and alternative strategies combining extended dosing schedules with T cell-directed adjuvant approaches will achieve superior seroconversion rates and antigen-specific T cell responses compared to conventional two-dose protocols.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-arm clinical study enrolling allogeneic transplant recipients and patients receiving anti-CD20 therapy (rituximab, obinutuzumab) across three UK NHS transplant centres. Participants will be randomized to: (1) standard two-dose vaccination, (2) extended three-dose primary series with prolonged intervals, (3) two doses plus heterologous boost with an adjuvanted platform, or (4) two doses supplemented with adoptive transfer of donor-derived virus-specific T cells. Immune responses will be assessed longitudinally using SARS-CoV-2 and influenza as model antigens, measuring binding and neutralizing antibody titres via ELISA and pseudovirus neutralization assays, B cell reconstitution by flow cytometry, and antigen-specific T cell responses by ELISpot and intracellular cytokine staining at baseline, 4 weeks, 3 months, and 6 months post-vaccination.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first head-to-head comparison of alternative vaccination strategies specifically designed for B cell-depleted patients, generating evidence to inform clinical guidelines for post-transplant immunization. The results will establish whether T cell-directed approaches can compensate for absent humoral immunity and define minimum immunological thresholds predictive of clinical protection in immunocompromised hosts.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have documented poor vaccine responses in immunosuppressed patients, none have systematically compared extended dosing, heterologous boosting, and adoptive T cell transfer as parallel alternative strategies within a single trial framework, nor established whether cellular immunity alone can serve as a correlate of protection when B cell reconstitution is absent.

proposals.title: Immunological Rescue by Third SARS-CoV-2 Vaccine Dose in Allogeneic Transplant Recipients and Patients on Immunosuppressive Therapy: A Prospective Cohort Study of Humoral and Cellular Responses
proposals.motivation: Patients with immune-suppressive conditions, including allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplant (allo-HSCT) recipients and those on B-cell-depleting or calcineurin-inhibitor therapies, mount significantly blunted responses to standard two-dose COVID-19 vaccination, leaving them vulnerable to severe SARS-CoV-2 infection. Prior studies from 2021-2022 reported seroconversion rates as low as 20-40% in these populations after two doses. Understanding whether a third dose can rescue both antibody and T-cell immunity in defined immunosuppressed subgroups is critical for evidence-based vaccination policy.
proposals.hypothesis: A third dose of BNT162b2 or mRNA-1273 administered at least 2 months after the second dose will significantly increase anti-spike IgG seroconversion rates and SARS-CoV-2-specific T-cell responses (measured by interferon-gamma ELISpot) in immunosuppressed patients who failed to seroconvert after two doses, with response magnitude varying by immunosuppressive regimen and time from transplant.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multicentre cohort study enrolling 200 immunosuppressed adults across four strata: allo-HSCT recipients within 12 months of transplant, allo-HSCT recipients beyond 12 months, solid organ transplant recipients on calcineurin inhibitors, and patients receiving anti-CD20 therapy for haematological malignancies. Participants who are seronegative (anti-spike IgG <7.1 BAU/mL by Roche Elecsys) after two mRNA vaccine doses will receive a third homologous dose. Blood samples collected at baseline, day 28, and day 90 post-third dose will be assayed for anti-spike IgG titres, neutralising antibody levels against wild-type and variants of concern via pseudovirus neutralisation, and spike-specific T-cell responses by IFN-gamma ELISpot and intracellular cytokine staining for CD4/CD8 subsets. Multivariable logistic regression will identify predictors of seroconversion, including lymphocyte count, immunoglobulin levels, time from transplant, and concurrent immunosuppressive agents.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide stratified evidence on which immunosuppressed subpopulations benefit most from a third vaccine dose, directly informing clinical guidelines for booster timing and patient prioritisation. The paired humoral-cellular analysis will clarify whether T-cell rescue can occur even when antibody responses remain suboptimal, offering a more complete picture of vaccine-mediated protection in vulnerable cohorts.
proposals.novelty: Unlike earlier third-dose studies that aggregated heterogeneous immunosuppressed populations, this work stratifies by specific regimen and transplant timing while simultaneously profiling both neutralising antibody breadth against variants and multi-parameter T-cell functionality, enabling identification of discordant humoral-cellular rescue patterns that prior studies overlooked.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Assessment of Cross-Reactive SARS-CoV-2 T Cell Durability in Allogeneic Hematopoietic Cell Transplant Recipients Against Omicron Subvariants
proposals.motivation: Immunosuppressed patients, particularly allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplant (allo-HCT) recipients, remain at high risk for severe COVID-19 despite vaccination. While pre-2026 studies have shown that cross-reactive T cell responses to Omicron variants can be detected shortly after vaccination in some immunocompromised cohorts, the long-term durability of these responses beyond six months is poorly characterized. This gap is critical for informing revaccination schedules and understanding the risk of breakthrough infections in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that cross-reactive memory T cell responses against SARS-CoV-2 Omicron subvariants (BA.2, BA.4/BA.5) in allo-HCT recipients will wane significantly between 6 and 12 months post-vaccination, correlating with time post-transplant, degree of immunosuppression, and graft-versus-host disease status.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal cohort study of 80 allo-HCT recipients vaccinated against SARS-CoV-2. Peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) will be collected at 1, 6, 9, and 12 months post-completion of primary vaccine series. Cross-reactive T cell responses will be quantified using high-parameter flow cytometry (intracellular cytokine staining for IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2) and activation-induced marker (AIM) assays following stimulation with peptide megapools spanning the spike proteins of the ancestral (Wuhan) strain and Omicron subvariants (BA.2, BA.5). Clinical metadata (transplant details, immunosuppressive regimens, lymphocyte counts) will be analyzed using mixed-effects regression models to identify predictors of response durability.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed longitudinal map of cross-reactive T cell durability in allo-HCT recipients, a key population in Dr. Richardson's research portfolio. It will directly inform evidence-based revaccination policies for transplant centers and identify clinical factors associated with sustained immune protection, which could guide prophylactic strategies. The findings will contribute to the broader understanding of adaptive immunity in the context of persistent viral variants and profound immunosuppression.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has characterized initial cross-reactivity, this proposal focuses on the critical, unaddressed question of long-term durability (>6 months) specifically in allo-HCT patients, integrating detailed immunophenotyping with granular clinical transplant data to uncover mechanistic drivers of waning immunity.

proposals.title: Model-Informed Precision Dosing of Immunosuppressants to Augment Vaccine Immunogenicity in Allogeneic Hematopoietic Cell Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: Allogeneic transplant patients on chronic immunosuppression, such as calcineurin inhibitors (e.g., tacrolimus), have impaired vaccine responses, as highlighted by studies of COVID-19 vaccination. Current guidelines for dose modification around vaccination are empirical and non-personalized, risking either suboptimal immunogenicity or increased risk of graft-versus-host disease (GVHD). A pharmacokinetically-guided strategy is needed to rationally balance immunosuppression and vaccine efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using population pharmacokinetic (PopPK) modeling and Bayesian forecasting to guide precise, temporary reductions in calcineurin inhibitor exposure around vaccination will enhance antigen-specific T- and B-cell responses without increasing the short-term risk of acute GVHD.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study in adult allogeneic transplant recipients on tacrolimus. Serial blood samples will be collected to build a PopPK model using NONMEM. This model will inform a Bayesian estimator to predict individualized drug clearance. For the intervention cohort, tacrolimus doses will be adjusted pre- and post-vaccination to achieve a target 25% reduction in area-under-the-curve (AUC) for a 7-day period, compared to standard care controls. Primary outcomes will be antigen-specific IgG titers and IFN-γ ELISpot responses 4 weeks post-vaccination, with GVHD incidence monitored for 90 days.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first clinical evidence for a model-informed dosing protocol to improve vaccine efficacy in transplant patients. It will deliver a practical, personalized medicine tool (a validated PopPK model with Bayesian dosing guidelines) for use in survivorship care. The findings could directly inform national transplant and vaccination policy.
proposals.novelty: While PopPK models are standard for preventing transplant rejection, their application to temporarily modulate drug exposure for enhancing vaccine responses is novel. This approach moves beyond empirical dose holds to a precision framework that quantitatively links pharmacokinetics, immune cell function, and clinical risk.

proposals.title: A Global Immunosuppressed Patient Registry (GIPR): Characterizing Humoral and Cellular Immune Responses to Diverse Vaccines Across Immune-Suppressive Diseases and Therapies
proposals.motivation: Patients with immune-suppressive diseases (e.g., hematologic cancers, post-transplant) or on immunosuppressive therapies remain at high risk for severe infections and suboptimal vaccine responses, as highlighted by the COVID-19 pandemic. The pre-2026 OCTAVE cohort demonstrated heterogeneity in immune responses to SARS-CoV-2 vaccines, but a comprehensive, global registry spanning multiple vaccine types and diverse immunosuppressive conditions is lacking to inform risk stratification and personalized vaccination strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific immunosuppressive conditions (e.g., B-cell malignancies, post-allogeneic transplant) and therapies (e.g., anti-CD20, calcineurin inhibitors) will be associated with distinct, predictable patterns of humoral and T-cell vaccine response failure across different vaccine platforms (viral vector, mRNA, inactivated).
proposals.method: We will expand the OCTAVE framework into a global, multicenter registry. Standardized protocols will collect longitudinal data on patient demographics, underlying disease, immunosuppressive regimen, vaccine history, and clinical outcomes. Core labs will perform centralized, harmonized immunophenotyping, including spike-specific IgG/Nab assays and interferon-gamma ELISpot/T-cell activation-induced marker assays. We will employ multivariable mixed-effects regression models to identify predictors of response, and unsupervised clustering algorithms (e.g., k-means on immune response trajectories) to define patient endotypes.
proposals.contribution: This will create the first large-scale, immunologically detailed resource for comparing vaccine immunogenicity across the spectrum of immunosuppression. It will generate evidence-based guidelines for vaccine timing, booster schedules, and the need for prophylactic antibodies specific to patient subgroups, directly impacting survivorship care for transplant and oncology patients.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies focus on single diseases or vaccines, this registry concurrently analyzes multiple vaccine platforms and diverse immunosuppressive etiologies, enabling direct comparison of risk factors and the discovery of shared versus unique pathways of immune compromise.

proposals.title: A Time-Resolved Multi-Parameter Flow Cytometry Benchmarking Platform to Establish a Composite Marker Panel for Robust Plasma Cell Identification in Multiple Myeloma Diagnostics
proposals.motivation: Accurate identification of plasma cells (PCs) is critical for diagnosing and monitoring multiple myeloma (MM) and its precursor states. The current gold standard marker, CD138, is known to undergo rapid antigenic shedding ex vivo, leading to false-negative results, while proposed alternatives like CD66a lack rigorous validation of their specificity across the disease spectrum. This creates a significant diagnostic gap, particularly for minimal residual disease (MRD) detection, where sensitivity is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite panel of PC markers, benchmarked against a histological ground truth and corrected for time-dependent degradation, will provide superior diagnostic sensitivity and specificity compared to any single marker, including CD138, across the MM disease continuum.
proposals.method: We will employ a split-sample design using fresh bone marrow aspirates from patients with normal PCs, MGUS, smoldering myeloma, and active MM. Matched aliquots will be processed and stained at defined intervals (0, 1, 2, 4, 8 hours) post-collection. Multi-parameter flow cytometry will simultaneously quantify expression levels and degradation kinetics of CD138, CD38, CD319/SLAMF7, VS38c, and CD66a. Diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity) for each marker and timepoint will be benchmarked against paired, fresh bone marrow biopsy immunohistochemistry as the ground truth. Statistical analysis will generate time-adjusted positivity thresholds and a composite scoring algorithm.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first empirically validated, time-adjusted composite marker panel for PC identification, directly addressing the pre-analytical variability of CD138. It will provide diagnostic laboratories with an evidence-based, standardized protocol to improve the accuracy of MM diagnosis and MRD assessment, ultimately enhancing patient risk stratification and treatment monitoring.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated, time-resolved benchmarking framework that simultaneously quantifies marker degradation and diagnostic performance against a histological standard, moving beyond single-marker comparisons to generate a robust, composite diagnostic tool validated across the entire MM disease spectrum.

proposals.title: A Bispecific AND-Gate Targeting CEACAM1 and BCMA for Selective Myeloma Cytotoxicity: Preclinical Validation in Patient-Derived Models
proposals.motivation: Multiple myeloma remains incurable for many patients, with relapse after BCMA-targeted therapies like CAR-T cells and antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) being a major challenge. CEACAM1 is a promising but broadly expressed antigen; targeting it alone risks on-target, off-tumor toxicity in CEACAM1+ normal tissues, including hematopoietic cells and epithelia. This fatal selectivity problem necessitates novel strategies to confine cytotoxicity exclusively to malignant plasma cells.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a bispecific AND-gate construct requiring simultaneous engagement of CEACAM1 and BCMA will achieve potent and selective killing of myeloma cells co-expressing both antigens, while sparing normal tissues expressing only CEACAM1.
proposals.method: We will engineer a split-ADC or conditional T-cell engager (TCE) architecture where effector function (e.g., payload release or CD3 engagement) is conditionally activated only upon dual antigen binding. Using flow cytometry, we will first quantify CEACAM1/BCMA co-expression on CD138+ plasma cells from patient bone marrow aspirates (n=20) and compare it to normal donor hematopoietic cells. The lead construct will be tested in vitro for cytotoxicity and specificity using co-culture assays with target cell lines and primary samples. In vivo efficacy and safety will be assessed in immunodeficient NSG mice engrafted with primary BCMA+/CEACAM1+ myeloma cells, comparing the AND-gate to single-target BCMA and CEACAM1 controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a preclinical proof-of-concept for a novel, logic-gated therapeutic strategy to safely target CEACAM1 in myeloma. It will deliver critical data on the therapeutic window afforded by CEACAM1/BCMA co-expression and establish a blueprint for improving selectivity in allogeneic transplant and cellular therapy contexts by sparing CEACAM1+ normal cells.
proposals.novelty: While bispecific TCEs and ADCs for BCMA exist pre-2026, applying an AND-gate logic to pair BCMA with the non-lineage-restricted CEACAM1 is novel. This approach uniquely seeks to convert the liability of broad CEACAM1 expression into a precision tool by making its targeting conditional on a second, disease-enriched antigen.

proposals.title: A Phase 0/1 Imaging and Dosimetry Study of a Pre-targeted Anti-CEACAM1 Radioimmunotherapy Strategy for High-Risk Multiple Myeloma
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in allogeneic transplant and targeted therapies, patients with high-risk or relapsed multiple myeloma have poor outcomes, underscoring a need for novel, tumor-specific cytotoxic approaches. CD66a/CEACAM1 is a promising target due to its high and consistent expression on malignant plasma cells, but its presence on some normal tissues poses a challenge for conventional radioimmunotherapy. Adapting the established clinical development pathway of HER2-targeted radioimmunoconjugates offers a strategic framework to safely and effectively target CEACAM1 in myeloma.
proposals.hypothesis: A bispecific antibody pre-targeting strategy, validated by prior imaging with a diagnostic radionuclide conjugate, will enable the safe and effective delivery of therapeutic radiation to CEACAM1-positive myeloma cells in the bone marrow while minimizing off-target toxicity.
proposals.method: The study will first conjugate a humanized anti-CEACAM1 monoclonal antibody to zirconium-89 for PET/CT imaging to quantify biodistribution and confirm preferential bone marrow uptake in patients. Following validation, a bispecific antibody (anti-CEACAM1 × anti-DOTA-hapten) will be administered, and after optimal blood clearance, a lutetium-177-labeled DOTA-hapten will be injected. Individualized dosimetry based on the imaging phase will guide therapeutic dose escalation in a subsequent cohort, with safety and preliminary efficacy (e.g., minimal residual disease by flow cytometry) as endpoints.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first clinical proof-of-concept for CEACAM1 as a viable radioimmunotherapy target in myeloma. It will generate critical data on the pharmacokinetics and safety profile of a novel pre-targeting approach in a hematologic malignancy, potentially creating a new therapeutic option for transplant-ineligible or high-risk patients. The study will also provide a methodological blueprint for translating radioimmunotherapy to shared antigens in the bone marrow niche.
proposals.novelty: While pre-targeting strategies have been explored in solid tumors, their application to a hematologic malignancy like myeloma, and specifically targeting CEACAM1, is novel.
