This proposal systematically adapts the entire clinical translation pipeline from HER2-positive breast cancer, a non-obvious cross-disciplinary analogy, to solve the specific challenge of on-target, off-tumor toxicity in the marrow microenvironment.

proposals.title: A CEACAM1-Primed, Logic-Gated CAR-T Cell Platform to Overcome CD138 Shedding in Multiple Myeloma
proposals.motivation: In multiple myeloma, CD138 (syndecan-1) is the primary target for CAR-T therapy and diagnostic flow cytometry, but its active shedding from the plasma cell surface during sample processing and disease progression compromises both therapeutic targeting and minimal residual disease (MRD) monitoring. This creates a critical vulnerability in current treatment paradigms, leading to false-negative diagnostics and potential therapeutic escape. A stable, non-shedding surface antigen is urgently needed to anchor both cell therapy and response assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: Engineering CAR-T cells with a primary anti-CEACAM1 recognition domain, supplemented by a logic-gated CD138-binding domain, will yield a therapeutic product with more consistent target engagement and superior in vivo efficacy against myeloma, while enabling reliable post-transplant monitoring via CEACAM1-based flow cytometry.
proposals.method: We will construct a dual-target, AND-gate CAR-T cell using a 2nd-generation CAR architecture (CD3ζ/4-1BB). The primary, high-affinity scFv will target CEACAM1, while a secondary, lower-affinity CD138-binding scFv will be incorporated via a synthetic Notch (synNotch) receptor system to provide specificity gating. CAR-T cells will be generated from allogeneic, healthy-donor T cells using lentiviral transduction. Function will be validated in vitro using myeloma cell lines and primary patient samples with known CD138 shedding, and in vivo using established murine xenograft models of myeloma. Post-treatment monitoring will employ multi-parameter flow cytometry panels comparing CEACAM1 versus CD138 for detecting residual malignant plasma cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, more reliable CAR-T platform for multiple myeloma that directly addresses a fundamental biological limitation of the field's dominant target. It will establish CEACAM1 as a robust therapeutic and diagnostic anchor for plasma cell malignancies. Furthermore, it will provide a validated monitoring strategy to improve the assessment of therapeutic response and MRD in allogeneic transplant and CAR-T cell recipients.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in making the instability of the conventional target (CD138) the central design principle, using CEACAM1's membrane stability as the primary therapeutic driver rather than as a secondary or alternative target. The fusion of a synNotch-gated CD138 domain to a CEACAM1-CAR creates a unique logic-gated system specifically engineered to circumvent a pre-analytical and biological artifact.

proposals.title: Allogeneic CEACAM1-targeting CAR-T Cell Therapy for High-Risk Multiple Myeloma: A Preclinical and Phase I Feasibility Study
proposals.motivation: Multiple myeloma remains incurable, with high relapse rates post-autologous stem cell transplant, especially in high-risk patients. Existing BCMA-targeting CAR-T therapies face challenges with antigen escape and manufacturing delays. CEACAM1 is a novel target, highly expressed on myeloma plasma cells and implicated in disease progression, but its potential in hematologic malignancies is unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CEACAM1 is a viable and potent target for CAR-T cell therapy in multiple myeloma, and that allogeneic, 'off-the-shelf' anti-CEACAM1 CAR-T cells derived from healthy donors will induce robust anti-myeloma cytotoxicity with an acceptable safety profile.
proposals.method: We will engineer allogeneic CAR-T cells using CRISPR-Cas9 to disrupt the TCR alpha constant (TRAC) locus to prevent GvHD, alongside a second-generation CAR construct (anti-CEACAM1 scFv, CD28 co-stimulatory, CD3ζ signaling domain). In vitro cytotoxicity will be assessed against primary myeloma cells and cell lines using flow cytometry and Incucyte real-time imaging. In vivo efficacy and safety will be evaluated in NSG mouse xenograft models, with a focus on cytokine release syndrome and on-target/off-tumor toxicity in CEACAM1-expressing tissues.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish CEACAM1 as a new therapeutic target in myeloma, expanding the antigen repertoire beyond BCMA. It will generate critical preclinical data on the feasibility and safety of allogeneic anti-CEACAM1 CAR-T cells, directly informing the design of a first-in-human clinical trial for high-risk or relapsed patients.
proposals.novelty: This project innovatively applies a solid-tumor CAR-T target (CEACAM1) to a hematologic malignancy and combines it with an allogeneic, CRISPR-engineered platform to address manufacturing and patient-specific limitations of autologous CAR-T therapies in the transplant context.

proposals.title: Integrating CEACAM1 Expression Atlas with Functional Dependency Screens to Prioritize Actionable Targets Across Hematologic Malignancies
proposals.motivation: CEACAM1 is an immune checkpoint molecule with variable expression across hematologic cancers, but its functional role as a driver versus a bystander marker remains poorly defined. This ambiguity hinders the rational development of anti-CEACAM1 therapies, risking futile clinical trials in unresponsive disease contexts. A systematic, multi-modal assessment is urgently needed to distinguish which malignancies are truly dependent on CEACAM1 for survival or immune evasion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CEACAM1 expression alone is insufficient to predict therapeutic vulnerability, and that integrating expression profiling with functional genetic and pharmacologic perturbation will identify specific hematologic malignancy subtypes where CEACAM1 is an essential, actionable target.
proposals.method: We will first establish a CEACAM1 expression atlas using RNA-seq data from public cohorts (e.g., TCGA, TARGET) and validate with flow cytometry and tissue microarrays on patient samples spanning AML, ALL, CLL, DLBCL, MDS, and myeloma. In parallel, we will perform CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screens in a panel of patient-derived cell lines and patient-derived xenograft (PDX) models, correlating CEACAM1 loss with proliferation and apoptosis. Functional dependency will be further tested using anti-CEACAM1 blocking antibodies in vitro and in vivo. Statistical integration will use multivariate regression to correlate expression levels with dependency scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a definitive, functionally annotated map of CEACAM1 roles across hematologic cancers, directly informing clinical trial design by identifying biomarker-defined patient subsets most likely to respond. It will establish a generalizable pipeline for moving from descriptive expression studies to mechanistically grounded target prioritization in oncology.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies catalog CEACAM1 expression, this project uniquely couples comprehensive expression mapping with direct functional validation using CRISPR and antibody blockade across a broad malignancy spectrum, explicitly aiming to pre-emptively identify and deprioritize non-dependent contexts for therapeutic development.

proposals.title: Oral Lipid Nanoparticle Delivery of Anti-CEACAM1 Nanobodies: A Novel Immunotherapeutic Strategy for CEACAM1-Positive Hematologic Malignancies
proposals.motivation: Monoclonal antibody therapies for hematologic malignancies are highly effective but require parenteral administration, posing a significant burden for patients, especially in long-term or post-transplant settings. CEACAM1 (CD66a) is a validated immunomodulatory surface antigen implicated in the pathogenesis of several leukemias and lymphomas, making it a promising target. Developing an orally bioavailable antibody fragment could revolutionize patient care by enabling convenient, self-administered immunotherapy, aligning with the need for improved survivorship and reduced treatment-related morbidity in transplant and immunocompromised patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CEACAM1 is consistently overexpressed across a defined subset of hematologic malignancies and that anti-CEACAM1 single-domain antibody fragments (nanobodies), when encapsulated in engineered lipid nanoparticles (LNPs), can be orally delivered to achieve systemic bioavailability sufficient for target engagement and anti-tumor efficacy in preclinical models.
proposals.method: First, we will perform a comprehensive CEACAM1 expression survey using flow cytometry and immunohistochemistry on primary patient samples and cell lines representing major hematologic malignancies. For formulation, we will design LNPs using ionizable lipids (e.g., DLin-MC3-DMA), phospholipids, cholesterol, and PEG-lipids, optimized via a design-of-experiments approach for high nanobody encapsulation efficiency and intestinal absorption. The LNPs will be surface-functionalized with targeting ligands (e.g., FcRn-binding peptides) to enhance transcytosis. Pharmacokinetic studies in murine models will assess oral bioavailability, followed by efficacy testing in established CEACAM1-positive xenograft models of leukemia and lymphoma.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the therapeutic potential of CEACAM1 as a broad target in hematologic oncology and provide the first proof-of-concept for an orally delivered antibody fragment against a hematologic malignancy. It will generate a novel LNP formulation platform tailored for the oral delivery of protein therapeutics, directly addressing a major limitation in current biologic drug delivery.
proposals.novelty: While LNPs are established for nucleic acid delivery (e.g., siRNA, mRNA vaccines), their application for the oral delivery of functional antibody fragments is highly innovative. Fusing this advanced delivery technology with the targeting specificity of nanobodies creates a entirely new modality for cancer immunotherapy, moving beyond injectable-only formats.

proposals.title: Single-cell multi-omic dissection of CEACAM heterogeneity links germline microsatellite haplotypes to clonal architecture in hereditary myeloid malignancies
proposals.motivation: Distinguishing hereditary from sporadic myeloid malignancies, particularly in adults with variants of uncertain significance in genes like those in Fanconi anemia (FA) pathways, remains a clinical challenge with implications for transplant donor selection and familial screening. Pre-2026 studies show CEACAM (CD66) family members are aberrantly expressed in AML and modulate immune responses, but their heterogeneity and link to germline risk are unexplored. Simultaneously, microsatellite markers offer a rapid, cost-effective method for haplotype phasing and inferring germline predisposition backgrounds in bulk studies, yet have not been integrated with single-cell resolution phenotyping.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific germline microsatellite haplotypes, indicative of hereditary predisposition syndromes, are associated with the expansion of distinct malignant clones characterized by unique CEACAM family expression profiles and splice variant usage.
proposals.method: We will perform single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics 5' gene expression with feature barcoding for surface protein) on bone marrow samples from AML/myeloma patients with known germline status, stratified by microsatellite genotyping at loci linked to FA complementation groups and other syndromes (e.g., D13S317, D13S1701). Bioinformatic analysis will use Cell Ranger for alignment, Seurat for clustering and differential expression of CEACAM genes (CEACAM1, 5, 6, 8), and SCENIC for regulatory network inference. Germline haplotypes will be overlaid onto single-cell clusters to identify associations between microsatellite-defined risk backgrounds and specific CEACAM-high subpopulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel combinatorial biomarker framework pairing germline microsatellite architecture with tumor-intrinsic CEACAM heterogeneity, providing a tool for stratifying hereditary risk at diagnosis. It will reveal mechanistic insights into how DNA repair deficiencies may dysregulate CEACAMs, potentially identifying new surface targets for immunotherapy or antibody-drug conjugates in predisposition-associated clones.
proposals.novelty: This is the first study to integrate microsatellite-based germline haplotyping with single-cell transcriptomic profiling of CEACAMs, moving beyond bulk genetic associations to link hereditary risk architecture directly to intra-tumor immunophenotypic heterogeneity.

proposals.title: Disease-Specific Variation in Hematopoietic Stem Cell Mobilization Efficacy of Biosimilar G-CSFs: A Multi-Center Clinical Study in Allogeneic Transplant Donors
proposals.motivation: Biosimilar granulocyte colony-stimulating factors (G-CSFs) are widely used for hematopoietic stem cell mobilization prior to allogeneic transplantation, with approval based on equivalence studies in healthy donors. However, patients with hematological malignancies have heterogeneous disease biology and prior treatment exposures (e.g., post-tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy). Pre-2026 clinical data suggests variable mobilization success, but systematic analysis of biosimilar efficacy across distinct disease subtypes is lacking, posing a potential risk to transplant logistics and donor safety.
proposals.hypothesis: The clinical equivalence of biosimilar G-CSFs to originator products for stem cell mobilization is not uniform but varies significantly according to the underlying hematological disease subtype and prior treatment history of the donor.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational multi-center cohort study of related donors undergoing mobilization with a single biosimilar G-CSF (e.g., filgrastim). Donors will be stratified by the recipient's disease subtype (e.g., acute lymphoblastic leukemia with ABL-class fusion, other ALL, AML, myelodysplastic syndrome). The primary endpoint is CD34+ cell yield per kg recipient weight after a standard mobilization course. Secondary endpoints include time to target yield and incidence of poor mobilizers. We will use multivariable regression modeling, adjusting for donor age, sex, and prior therapy (including specific risk agents like TKIs), to test for disease-specific differences in mobilization efficacy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust clinical evidence for or against disease-specific biosimilar G-CSF efficacy, directly informing donor selection and mobilization protocols. It will add a critical layer of precision to survivorship care for transplant donors and improve the reliability of stem cell harvests, impacting clinical practice and health service policy for transplant centers.
proposals.novelty: While biosimilar equivalence is assumed class-wide, testing this assumption across the specific disease contexts relevant to allogeneic transplant—particularly for donors who are patients themselves—is a novel and clinically urgent question not addressed in prior approval studies.

proposals.title: Functional Graft Quality Assessment Beyond CD34+ Enumeration: A Comparative Study of Biosimilar vs. Originator G-CSF in Hematopoietic Stem Cell Mobilization
proposals.motivation: The increasing use of biosimilar granulocyte colony-stimulating factors (G-CSFs) for stem cell mobilization in allogeneic transplant relies on CD34+ cell count as the primary efficacy endpoint. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests CD34+ count may not capture functional graft properties critical for engraftment speed, immune reconstitution, and long-term outcomes. This is a significant clinical risk, as subtle functional deficits could impact transplant success, particularly in high-risk patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hematopoietic progenitor cell (HPC) grafts mobilized with biosimilar G-CSF, despite meeting CD34+ count adequacy thresholds, will exhibit functional deficits in progenitor quality, homing potential, and immunomodulatory capacity compared to those mobilized with the originator product.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study of matched related donor-recipient pairs. For grafts mobilized with biosimilar (e.g., filgrastim-sndz) or originator (filgrastim) G-CSF, we will perform deep functional profiling alongside CD34+ enumeration. This will include in vitro colony-forming unit (CFU) assays, flow cytometric analysis of primitive HPC subsets (CD34+CD38-, CD34+CD90+), measurement of CXCR4 expression for homing, and ex vivo T-cell suppression assays using co-cultured CD34-derived myeloid cells to assess immunomodulatory function. Clinical outcomes (time to neutrophil/platelet engraftment, incidence of acute GVHD) will be correlated with laboratory parameters using multivariate regression analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive functional comparison of HPC grafts mobilized by biosimilar versus originator G-CSF in a clinical setting. It will establish a multi-parameter framework for graft quality assessment beyond CD34+ count, informing clinical guidelines for biosimilar use and potentially identifying patients who may benefit from augmented graft evaluation or post-transplant monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While biosimilar clinical efficacy is accepted based on CD34+ counts, direct comparative analysis of functional graft properties—specifically immunomodulatory capacity linked to transplant complications—is lacking. This proposal moves beyond numerical adequacy to interrogate the biological equivalence of mobilized grafts in a transplant-relevant context.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Randomized, Controlled Trial Comparing Biosimilar and Originator Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor for Hematopoietic Stem Cell Mobilization in Allogeneic Transplant Donors
proposals.motivation: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) is critical for mobilizing peripheral blood stem cells from healthy donors for allogeneic transplantation. While biosimilar G-CSFs are widely used to reduce costs, the evidence for their equivalence to originator products in this specific, healthy donor population is largely retrospective or based on small, non-randomized studies. A high-quality, prospective randomized trial is needed to definitively inform clinical practice and health service policy, ensuring donor safety and optimal cell yield without compromising transplant outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that biosimilar G-CSF is non-inferior to the originator G-CSF in achieving target CD34+ cell yields for collection in healthy allogeneic stem cell donors, with no significant difference in donor safety profiles or post-transplant engraftment kinetics in recipients.
proposals.method: This will be a multicenter, prospective, randomized, double-blind, non-inferiority trial. Healthy donors for allogeneic transplant will be randomized 1:1 to receive either biosimilar filgrastim or originator filgrastim for mobilization. The primary endpoint is the proportion of donors achieving a target CD34+ cell yield (e.g., ≥4 x 10^6 CD34+ cells/kg recipient weight) in one apheresis session. Secondary endpoints include total CD34+ yield, engraftment times (neutrophil and platelet) in recipients, incidence of donor adverse events (e.g., bone pain), and health economic analysis. Statistical analysis will use a pre-defined non-inferiority margin for the primary endpoint, with intention-to-treat and per-protocol analyses.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide Level I evidence to guide the selection of G-CSF products for stem cell mobilization in healthy donors, directly impacting clinical guidelines and procurement policies. It will offer robust data on donor safety and product efficacy, addressing a key uncertainty in transplant logistics and potentially generating significant cost savings for healthcare systems like the NHS without compromising care.
proposals.novelty: While retrospective analyses exist, this is the first prospective, randomized, double-blind trial designed specifically to test the equivalence of biosimilar and originator G-CSF in the critical context of healthy donor mobilization for allogeneic transplant, a population distinct from patients undergoing autologous collection or chemotherapy.

proposals.title: Long-Term Survival, Relapse, and Late Engraftment Complications Following Biosimilar Filgrastim-Mobilized Allogeneic Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation: A Multicentre Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Biosimilar granulocyte colony-stimulating factors (GCSF) have been widely adopted for stem cell mobilization in allogeneic transplantation based on short-term equivalence data, yet robust evidence on long-term outcomes beyond two years remains sparse. Given that late graft failure, secondary malignancies, and chronic graft-versus-host disease may take years to manifest, the absence of extended follow-up data represents a critical gap in transplant safety evidence. With increasing NHS and global reliance on biosimilar filgrastim to reduce costs, establishing long-term outcome parity with originator GCSF is essential for patient safety and health policy.
proposals.hypothesis: Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplants mobilized with biosimilar filgrastim yield non-inferior five-year overall survival, cumulative relapse incidence, and rates of late engraftment complications compared to those mobilized with originator filgrastim, after adjusting for disease risk, conditioning intensity, and donor characteristics.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicentre retrospective cohort study across UK transplant centres using registry data from the British Society of Blood and Marrow Transplantation (BSBMT), comparing adult patients who received biosimilar versus originator filgrastim-mobilized allografts between 2013 and 2023. Primary endpoints include five-year overall survival and progression-free survival, analysed using Kaplan-Meier estimation and multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression adjusting for HCT-CI comorbidity index, disease risk index, conditioning regimen, donor type, and HLA matching. Competing risks analysis using Fine-Gray models will evaluate cumulative incidence of relapse, non-relapse mortality, and late graft failure. Propensity score matching will address selection bias in mobilization agent assignment, and sensitivity analyses will stratify by disease subtype and donor source.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, long-term comparative effectiveness analysis of biosimilar versus originator GCSF-mobilized allografts, directly informing transplant centre procurement decisions and national commissioning guidelines. The findings will either validate current cost-saving practices or identify specific patient subgroups requiring closer surveillance, contributing actionable evidence for survivorship care protocols aligned with JACIE accreditation standards.
proposals.novelty: While existing studies have demonstrated short-term bioequivalence of biosimilar filgrastim for mobilization, no adequately powered study has examined outcomes beyond three years or systematically assessed late engraftment complications including poor graft function and secondary graft failure. By linking mobilization agent data with extended registry follow-up and applying competing risks methodology, this work addresses a non-obvious gap where assumed equivalence has outpaced supporting evidence.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Randomized, Double-Blind Comparison of Biosimilar versus Reference Filgrastim for Hematopoietic Stem Cell Mobilization in Healthy Allogeneic Donors: Efficacy, Safety, and Donor Experience
proposals.motivation: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) is standard for mobilizing CD34+ cells from healthy donors for allogeneic hematopoietic cell transplantation (HCT). While biosimilar G-CSFs are established in patients, their use in healthy donors is less defined, despite significant cost-saving potential. Donors represent a unique population where minimizing adverse events and ensuring reliable yield is paramount, yet direct comparative data on biosimilar performance in this setting are sparse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that biosimilar filgrastim is non-inferior to the reference product in achieving target CD34+ cell yields in healthy allogeneic donors, with a comparable safety and tolerability profile.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-center, randomized, double-blind, phase IV non-inferiority trial. Healthy donors (n=120) will receive either reference filgrastim or an approved biosimilar at 10 µg/kg/day for 4-5 days. The primary endpoint is the total CD34+ cell yield per kg donor weight collected via apheresis. Secondary endpoints include mobilization kinetics (pre-apheresis CD34+ count), donor-reported outcomes (using validated quality-of-life and symptom burden questionnaires), incidence of common adverse events (bone pain, headache), and rates of donor attrition. Statistical analysis will employ a pre-specified non-inferiority margin for the primary endpoint.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence from a blinded, randomized trial directly comparing biosimilar and reference G-CSF in healthy donors. It will inform clinical guidelines and institutional policies on biosimilar adoption for donor mobilization, potentially reducing transplant costs without compromising donor safety or graft quality. The data will also enrich the understanding of donor experience and risk profiles with different G-CSF formulations.
proposals.novelty: While biosimilar G-CSF studies exist in patient populations, this work uniquely focuses on the distinct physiology and risk-benefit calculus of the healthy donor, a critical but understudied component of the allogeneic transplant ecosystem, applying rigorous clinical trial methodology to a question of significant practical and economic importance.

proposals.title: Comparative Immunogenicity and Adverse Event Profiles of Biosimilar versus Originator Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor in Allogeneic Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (GCSF) is critical for neutrophil recovery post-allogeneic stem cell transplant. Biosimilar GCSF formulations are widely adopted to reduce costs, but comprehensive, real-world data comparing their immunogenicity and adverse event (AE) profiles to the originator in this high-risk, immunocompromised patient population are limited. Given the known risks of immune-mediated complications post-transplant and the potential for anti-drug antibodies to affect efficacy or cause adverse events, a systematic comparison is urgently needed to inform clinical practice and safety monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that biosimilar and originator GCSF formulations will have comparable rates of anti-GCSF antibody formation and similar adverse event profiles in the post-transplant setting, supporting their therapeutic equivalence beyond pharmacokinetic studies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, observational cohort study of adult and pediatric patients receiving GCSF (biosimilar or originator) post-allogeneic transplant at a major UK transplant centre. Serum samples will be collected pre-treatment and at days +14, +28, and +100 post-transplant. Anti-GCSF IgG antibodies will be quantified using a validated, drug-tolerant bridging electrochemiluminescence immunoassay. Adverse events (focusing on bone pain, fever, and potential immune-mediated events) will be systematically captured via patient diaries and electronic health records, graded per CTCAE v5.0. Statistical analysis will employ multivariate logistic regression to compare immunogenicity and AE rates, adjusting for key confounders including underlying disease, conditioning regimen, and graft-versus-host disease prophylaxis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed, longitudinal immunogenicity data for GCSF biosimilars in the allogeneic transplant population, directly addressing a key safety concern. The findings will offer evidence-based guidance for clinicians and policymakers on the safety profile of biosimilar GCSF in a complex, immunologically vulnerable cohort, potentially influencing NHS procurement and JACIE-accredited centre protocols.
proposals.novelty: While biosimilar pharmacokinetic studies exist, this work uniquely focuses on the immunocompromised transplant recipient, a population at heightened risk for altered immunogenicity and complications, and integrates detailed immune monitoring with comprehensive real-world adverse event profiling not captured in registration trials.

proposals.title: Comparative Efficacy and Safety of Biosimilar versus Originator Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor for Hematopoietic Stem Cell Mobilization in Pediatric Allogeneic Transplant Donors
proposals.motivation: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) is critical for mobilizing stem cells from healthy donors for allogeneic transplantation. While biosimilar G-CSFs are established as cost-effective alternatives in adults, their equivalence in pediatric populations is not robustly proven. Children represent a pharmacologically distinct group with different pharmacokinetics, bone marrow reserve, and risk profiles, making direct extrapolation from adult data potentially unsafe and necessitating specific pediatric investigation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that biosimilar G-CSF is non-inferior to the originator product in achieving target CD34+ cell yields for pediatric donors, but will exhibit a distinct profile of efficacy kinetics and short-term adverse events due to age-related physiological differences.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multicenter, observational cohort study of pediatric sibling donors (aged 2-18 years) mobilized for allogeneic transplant. Donors receiving biosimilar G-CSF will be compared to a historical cohort matched for age, weight, and diagnosis receiving originator G-CSF. The primary endpoint is the CD34+ cell yield per kg of donor weight after the first apheresis. Secondary endpoints include mobilization kinetics (daily CD34+ counts), apheresis duration, and donor toxicity (using standardized pediatric pain and bone pain scales, and platelet nadir). Pharmacokinetic sub-studies will model drug clearance. Statistical analysis will employ a non-inferiority design with a pre-specified margin.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-quality, pediatric-specific evidence to guide the use of biosimilar G-CSF in stem cell mobilization, directly impacting clinical practice and health economic policies for transplant centers. It will generate novel data on mobilization kinetics and tolerability in children, informing donor counseling and risk stratification.
proposals.novelty: While biosimilar equivalence is accepted in adults, this study explicitly tests it in the physiologically and pharmacologically distinct pediatric population, a critical gap given the unique risks in young donors. It integrates pharmacokinetic analysis with clinical mobilization outcomes specifically for children, a approach not yet comprehensively reported.

proposals.title: A Phase II Trial of a TKI-Dominant, Chemotherapy-Sparing Regimen for Pediatric and Adolescent Patients with ABL-Class Fusion Positive B-ALL: Assessing Efficacy and Toxicity
proposals.motivation: For pediatric and adolescent patients with ABL-class fusion positive B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL), the standard of care combines intensive multi-agent chemotherapy with adjuvant tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), as shown in recent studies. While effective, this approach subjects patients, particularly those undergoing subsequent allogeneic stem cell transplant, to significant short- and long-term toxicities, including organ damage and increased risk of secondary malignancies. A strategy to reduce chemotherapy exposure could substantially improve survivorship outcomes and quality of life.
proposals.hypothesis: A treatment regimen where a potent, next-generation TKI (e.g., dasatinib or ponatinib) serves as the primary cytoreductive agent, supported by a minimized, non-myelosuppressive chemotherapy backbone, will achieve non-inferer minimal residual disease (MRD)-negative remission rates compared to standard chemo-TKI combination therapy, while significantly reducing the incidence and severity of treatment-related toxicities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a single-arm, multi-center Phase II trial. Eligible patients (aged 1-21 years with newly diagnosed ABL-class fusion positive B-ALL) will receive induction therapy with a TKI (selected based on fusion genotype and resistance profile) plus dexamethasone and vincristine only, omitting anthracyclines and alkylating agents. The primary endpoint is the rate of MRD-negative (<0.01%) complete remission at the end of induction, assessed by high-sensitivity flow cytometry and immunoglobulin/T-cell receptor gene rearrangement PCR. Secondary endpoints include toxicity profiles (graded by CTCAE v5.0), event-free survival, and metrics of transplant-related risk (e.g., organ function, infection rates) for those proceeding to allogeneic transplant.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first clinical evidence for the feasibility and efficacy of a chemotherapy-sparing paradigm in high-risk pediatric ALL. It will directly inform risk-adapted treatment strategies by quantifying the trade-off between anti-leukemic efficacy and reduced toxicity. The results could redefine the standard backbone for targeted therapy in fusion-driven leukemias, impacting future clinical trial design and survivorship care guidelines.
proposals.novelty: This proposal challenges the entrenched paradigm that TKIs are merely adjuncts to chemotherapy, positing they can be the dominant therapeutic modality. It moves beyond optimizing adjuvant TKI use (the focus of pre-2026 work) to fundamentally re-engineering the treatment backbone to prioritize reduced long-term morbidity.

proposals.title: Impact of Pre-Remission Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor Initiation on Early Molecular Response and Transplant Outcomes in Pediatric B-ALL with ABL-Class Fusions
proposals.motivation: For children and adolescents with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL) harboring ABL-class fusions, the current standard of care involves initiating tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) therapy after achieving morphological remission post-induction chemotherapy. This delay may allow for the persistence of a chemotherapy-resistant clone, potentially compromising early minimal residual disease (MRD) clearance—a critical prognostic factor for success in subsequent allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT). Optimizing pre-transplant disease control is paramount for improving long-term survival in this high-risk group.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that initiating TKI therapy concurrently with diagnosis and induction chemotherapy, rather than after remission assessment, will lead to superior rates of early MRD negativity and improved event-free survival post-allogeneic HSCT for pediatric patients with ABL-class fusion-positive B-ALL.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, phase II clinical trial comparing early (Day 1 of induction) versus standard (post-induction) initiation of adjuvant TKI (e.g., dasatinib) in newly diagnosed patients. The primary endpoint will be the rate of MRD negativity (<0.01%) by flow cytometry and immunoglobulin/T-cell receptor gene rearrangement PCR at the end of induction. Secondary endpoints will include MRD levels pre-transplant, transplant-related mortality, relapse incidence, and overall survival. Patient risk stratification will incorporate pre-existing comorbidities and immune reconstitution data, informed by Dr. Richardson's work on post-transplant outcomes and immune-suppressive disease.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective evidence defining the optimal timing of TKI integration in frontline therapy for this molecular subset. It will directly inform clinical practice by testing a strategy to deepen pre-transplant remission, potentially reducing relapse risk and improving the therapeutic benefit of allogeneic HSCT. The findings will also contribute to understanding the dynamics of early leukemia cell clearance under combined chemo-targeted therapy.
proposals.novelty: While adjuvant TKIs are known to improve outcomes, the novel aspect is challenging the paradigm of waiting for remission assessment, proactively using TKIs from diagnosis to manipulate the very early disease kinetics and MRD trajectory before transplant, a strategy not yet systematically evaluated in pediatric trials pre-2026.

proposals.title: Minimizing Toxicity While Preserving Efficacy: A Phase II Trial to Define the Optimal Duration and Dosing of Adjuvant Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor Therapy in Pediatric B-ALL with ABL-Class Fusions
proposals.motivation: Adjuvant TKI therapy has improved outcomes for children with ABL-class fusion B-ALL, but the optimal treatment intensity to prevent relapse remains undefined. Prolonged, standard-dose TKI exposure carries significant risks of long-term toxicities, growth impairment, and financial burden, which are critical concerns in a pediatric population. Defining the minimal effective regimen is therefore a major unmet need in survivorship and precision oncology for this subset of patients.
proposals.hypothesis: A reduced total exposure (lower dose and/or shorter duration) of adjuvant TKI, guided by early molecular response and pharmacokinetic monitoring, will be non-inferior to standard protocols in preventing relapse, while significantly reducing treatment-related toxicity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, randomized phase II trial. Patients in remission post-chemotherapy, with detectable ABL-class fusions, will be stratified by fusion type and randomized to control (standard TKI dose/duration) or experimental arms featuring stepwise dose reduction or truncated duration. The primary endpoint will be event-free survival. Secondary endpoints include toxicity profiles, quality-of-life measures, and pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) analysis. We will employ high-sensitivity droplet digital PCR (ddPCR) for minimal residual disease (MRD) monitoring at serial timepoints to correlate molecular response with TKI exposure. A Bayesian adaptive design will be used to efficiently identify the most promising de-escalation strategy.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first prospective evidence to guide TKI de-escalation in pediatric ALL, directly addressing survivorship concerns highlighted in transplant care. It will establish a framework for risk-adapted, minimal-intensity adjuvant therapy, reducing long-term side effects. The integrated PK/PD and MRD data will yield biomarkers to personalize TKI use, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all approach.
proposals.novelty: While recent work established the benefit of adding TKIs, this proposal proactively seeks to minimize them, applying a survivorship and precision medicine lens to a successful therapy. The use of a Bayesian adaptive trial design specifically for de-escalation and the integration of real-time MRD to guide dosing duration are novel methodological approaches in this context.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Assessment of Growth, Bone Metabolism, and Subclinical Organ Dysfunction in Pediatric Patients on Chronic Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor Therapy
proposals.motivation: Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) have revolutionized the treatment of pediatric leukemias, such as those with ABL-class fusions, leading to prolonged survival and increased use as adjuvant or maintenance therapy. However, their long-term impact on developing children remains poorly characterized, with pre-clinical and limited clinical data suggesting risks to growth plates, bone mineralization, and organ systems. Systematic, long-term toxicity monitoring is essential to inform survivorship care for this growing population of long-term survivors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that prolonged TKI exposure in children is associated with dose- and duration-dependent deficits in longitudinal growth, reduced bone mineral density, and subclinical markers of cardiac, renal, and hepatic dysfunction compared to age-matched controls.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of pediatric patients (<18 years at initiation) treated with TKIs (e.g., imatinib, dasatinib) for hematologic malignancies. We will extract longitudinal data on anthropometrics, dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) scans, and serial laboratory markers (e.g., cardiac troponin, NT-proBNP, eGFR, liver enzymes) from electronic health records. Growth trajectories and Z-scores will be modeled using mixed-effects regression. Associations between cumulative TKI dose, treatment duration, and toxicity outcomes will be analyzed using multivariable Cox proportional hazards and linear regression models, adjusting for demographics, primary disease, and prior therapies (e.g., allogeneic transplant).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, longitudinal profile of long-term toxicities associated with pediatric TKI use, quantifying risks to growth and bone health. It will generate evidence-based guidelines for monitoring parameters and intervals, directly informing the development of tailored survivorship care plans for this vulnerable population within pediatric oncology and transplant follow-up programs.
proposals.novelty: While TKIs' efficacy is established, this study uniquely focuses on their chronic, off-target effects on development and organ function in children, leveraging real-world longitudinal data and modern statistical modeling to move beyond anecdotal reports to quantified risk assessment.

proposals.title: Functional Precision Oncology for ABL-Class Leukemias: A Prospective Trial of Patient-Derived Organoid Drug Sensitivity Testing to Guide Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor Selection
proposals.motivation: For patients with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL) harboring ABL-class fusions, adjuvant tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) therapy improves outcomes, but optimal TKI selection is not standardized. Current practice relies on genetic profiling and clinical trial data, which may not predict individual patient response due to complex clonal heterogeneity and resistance mechanisms. This creates a critical need for a functional, patient-specific assay to guide therapy and potentially improve survival while minimizing toxicity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ex vivo drug sensitivity testing of primary patient-derived leukemia organoids to a panel of clinically relevant TKIs (e.g., imatinib, dasatinib, ponatinib) will accurately predict clinical response and identify the optimal TKI for individual patients with ABL-class fusion-positive B-ALL.
proposals.method: We will prospectively enroll pediatric and adult patients with newly diagnosed or relapsed/refractory ABL-class fusion B-ALL. Bone marrow or peripheral blood mononuclear cells will be used to establish patient-derived leukemia organoids using established 3D co-culture systems with mesenchymal stromal cells. Organoids will be subjected to high-throughput drug sensitivity and resistance testing (DSRT) against a TKI panel. Dose-response curves will be generated, and the drug with the lowest ex vivo LC50 (concentration lethal to 50% of cells) will be recommended as the optimal TKI. Clinical outcomes (minimal residual disease, event-free survival) will be correlated with ex vivo predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a clinically actionable pipeline for functional precision medicine in high-risk leukemia. It will provide direct evidence for the utility of organoid-based DSRT in personalizing TKI therapy, moving beyond genomic stratification. The results could inform new clinical guidelines for TKI selection and improve patient outcomes by rapidly identifying the most effective agent.
proposals.novelty: While organoid models exist, their direct, prospective application to guide real-time TKI selection for individual leukemia patients in a clinical trial setting is novel. This approach integrates functional pharmacotyping with standard-of-care decision-making, a significant advance over retrospective correlative studies.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Multicenter Validation of CD34+ Thresholds for Pre-emptive Plerixafor Use in Allogeneic Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Donors
proposals.motivation: Mobilization failure in allogeneic stem cell donors leads to procedural delays, increased costs, and donor morbidity. While retrospective studies have proposed peripheral blood CD34+ count thresholds to guide pre-emptive plerixafor use, these lack prospective validation. In the pre-2026 context of optimizing donor safety and resource utilization, a definitive trial is needed to establish evidence-based, standardized mobilization protocols across transplant centers.
proposals.hypothesis: Prospectively applying a pre-defined CD34+ threshold (e.g., <10-15 cells/μL on day 4 of G-CSF) to trigger plerixafor administration will significantly reduce the rate of mobilization failure (<2.0 x 10^6 CD34+ cells/kg collected) compared to standard rescue-based approaches, without increasing toxicity or cost.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multicenter, interventional cohort study. Allogeneic donors undergoing mobilization with G-CSF will have daily peripheral blood CD34+ counts measured by standardized flow cytometry. Donors with a CD34+ count below the pre-specified threshold on day 4 will receive pre-emptive plerixafor. The primary endpoint is the incidence of mobilization failure. Outcomes will be compared to a contemporaneous control cohort managed per center-specific rescue protocols. Statistical analysis will use chi-square tests and logistic regression to assess the primary endpoint and adjust for covariates like donor age and G-CSF dose.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-level evidence for a standardized, biomarker-driven algorithm to guide plerixafor use in healthy donors. It will directly inform clinical practice guidelines, potentially improving donor experience and ensuring more reliable stem cell product collection for transplant recipients. The data will also allow for a robust cost-effectiveness analysis of pre-emptive versus rescue strategies.
proposals.novelty: While thresholds have been suggested, this study represents the first prospective, multicenter validation of a CD34+-guided interventional algorithm in allogeneic donors, moving beyond retrospective correlation to direct clinical implementation and efficacy testing.

proposals.title: A Prospective, Randomized Study Comparing Patient-Reported Quality of Life in Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients Undergoing Pre-emptive Versus Delayed CD34+ Cell Re-mobilization for Poor Graft Function
proposals.motivation: Poor graft function (PGF) is a serious complication after allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT), leading to prolonged cytopenias, infection risk, and transfusion dependence. Current management often involves a delayed, reactive approach to donor cell re-mobilization and boost. While pre-emptive strategies using stem cell boosts are being explored to hasten hematologic recovery, their impact on patient-centered outcomes, specifically quality of life (QoL), remains formally unmeasured and is critical for evaluating the true clinical value of this intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pre-emptive re-mobilization strategy for incipient PGF will lead to significantly better patient-reported quality of life outcomes within the first 100 days post-transplant compared to a standard delayed strategy, due to faster hematologic recovery and reduced complication burden.
proposals.method: This will be a multi-center, randomized controlled trial. Adult patients undergoing allogeneic HSCT for hematologic malignancies will be screened for early signs of PGF (defined by specific, declining blood count criteria between days +21 and +28). Eligible patients will be randomized 1:1 to pre-emptive re-mobilization (leukapheresis and cryopreservation of donor CD34+ cells upon PGF diagnosis, with immediate infusion if counts do not improve within 7 days) or delayed strategy (standard supportive care until meeting severe, protocol-defined criteria for boost). The primary endpoint is the difference in mean Functional Assessment of Cancer Therapy-Bone Marrow Transplant (FACT-BMT) score at day +100. Secondary endpoints include time to platelet and neutrophil recovery, incidence of severe infections, and healthcare utilization. Analysis will use an intention-to-treat approach with linear mixed models for longitudinal QoL data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence quantifying the patient-experience benefits of a pre-emptive cellular therapy strategy in transplant, moving beyond purely biomedical endpoints. It will inform clinical guidelines by integrating patient-reported outcomes into the risk-benefit assessment of early intervention for PGF. The findings will also contribute to survivorship care models by identifying modifiable transplant procedures that impact early recovery quality.
proposals.novelty: While prior research by Richardson and others has focused on clinical outcomes and service delivery in transplant, this proposal uniquely applies a rigorous patient-reported outcome methodology to evaluate a pre-emptive cellular intervention, directly linking a technical transplant strategy to quantifiable patient quality-of-life metrics.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Adaptive Trial to Optimize Plerixafor Dosing and Timing for Hematopoietic Stem Cell Mobilization in Allogeneic Transplant Recipients
proposals.motivation: Plerixafor is a key agent for mobilizing hematopoietic stem cells from donors for allogeneic transplantation, but its dosing and timing relative to G-CSF remain suboptimal, leading to variable CD34+ cell yields and increased donor discomfort or procedural delays. Current fixed-dose strategies do not account for inter-individual variability in factors like donor age, weight, or baseline cell counts, which can impact transplant success and patient outcomes. An adaptive, data-driven approach is needed to personalize mobilization protocols and improve efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian adaptive dose-finding design, which dynamically updates dosing recommendations based on real-time CD34+ yield and safety data, will identify a superior, personalized plerixafor dosing strategy compared to standard fixed dosing, leading to more consistent and optimal stem cell collection.
proposals.method: We will design a Phase II, multi-center trial using a Bayesian optimal interval (BOIN) design or a continual reassessment method (CRM) variant adapted for a bivariate efficacy-toxicity outcome. The primary efficacy endpoint will be CD34+ cell yield (target > 5 x 10^6/kg), and the primary safety endpoint will be the incidence of significant thrombocytopenia or other grade ≥3 adverse events. The model will incorporate donor covariates (age, weight, baseline platelet count) as adaptive modifiers. Dosing and timing decisions (e.g., plerixafor administration hours post-G-CSF) will be updated after each cohort using posterior probabilities to guide the next cohort's regimen.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, validated adaptive trial framework for stem cell mobilization, directly applicable to transplant center practice. It is expected to yield a personalized dosing algorithm that maximizes successful collection while minimizing donor risk and resource use. The findings will advance the methodology for optimizing supportive care agents in transplantation, a critical component of survivorship and outcomes for allogeneic recipients.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian adaptive designs are established in oncology drug development, their application to optimize the dosing of a mobilization agent like plerixafor, integrating real-time donor characteristics and yield data, is novel. This moves beyond the traditional 'one-size-fits-all' paradigm in transplant supportive care towards a precision medicine approach.

ideas.title: Oral bioavailability optimization of a repurposed drug for adult-onset Fanconi anemia without classic phenotype
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to develop and test advanced oral formulations (e.g., lipid-based, modified-release) to enable the effective repurposing of isoxsuprine, or a closely related vasodilator, for the prevention or treatment of myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS) and acute myeloid leukemia (AML) in adult patients with molecularly diagnosed Fanconi anemia (FA) who lack the classic congenital phenotype. The synergy arises from targeting a specific, vulnerable clinical niche—adults whose FA manifests primarily as hematologic malignancy—with a drug that has proposed preclinical rationale for FA pathway modulation but historically poor bioavailability. Success would provide a feasible, patient-compliant oral therapeutic option for long-term management in a population where current bone marrow transplant and intensive chemotherapy are highly problematic due to underlying genomic instability.

ideas.title: Borrowing from HIV-adapted oncology: dose-attenuated, DNA-repair-aware chemotherapy for FA-AML
ideas.description: Fanconi anemia (FA) patients who develop AML face a critical therapeutic dilemma: standard AML induction regimens (e.g., 7+3 cytarabine/daunorubicin) cause devastating toxicity due to the underlying DNA repair deficiency, yet undertreating risks leukemia progression. This problem closely parallels the challenge faced in HIV-associated oncology, where immunocompromised patients with lymphomas could not tolerate full-dose CHOP or hyper-CVAD regimens. The HIV-oncology field resolved this through response-adapted protocols such as dose-adjusted EPOCH-R, which employs continuous-infusion chemotherapy with real-time dose titration based on neutrophil nadir, allowing each patient's tolerance to guide escalation or de-escalation. By adapting this paradigm, we propose a FA-AML chemotherapy framework that initiates treatment at 50-60% standard intensity using agents with favorable toxicity profiles in DNA-repair-deficient cells (e.g., CPX-351 liposomal daunorubicin-cytarabine, which has shown improved outcomes in secondary AML), then adjusts doses cycle-by-cycle based on marrow recovery time, DEB-induced chromosomal breakage rates as a pharmacodynamic biomarker, and minimal residual disease response. To compensate for reduced cytotoxic intensity, the regimen incorporates molecularly targeted agents—venetoclax (BCL-2 inhibitor), gilteritinib (FLT3 inhibitor where applicable), and potentially gemtuzumab ozogamicin (anti-CD33 antibody-drug conjugate at reduced dosing)—mirroring how HIV-lymphoma protocols leveraged rituximab and novel agents to maintain efficacy at lower chemotherapy doses. This cross-disciplinary approach transforms FA-AML treatment from a binary choice between dangerous full-dose therapy and inadequate palliation into a precision-guided, tolerance-adaptive protocol.

ideas.title: MSC therapy for chronic GvHD prevention
ideas.description: Investigate whether prophylactic MSC infusions at transplant can prevent chronic GvHD, inverting the treatment-to-prevention paradigm.
ideas.sourceTitle: Mesenchymal stromal cells for acute graft‐versus‐host disease: response at 1 week predicts probability of survival

ideas.title: CyTOF-guided adaptive chemotherapy dosing for Fanconi anemia AML via CD66 variant immunophenotyping
ideas.description: This fused approach applies CyTOF mass cytometry to comprehensively profile CD66 (CEACAM) variant expression on AML blasts from Fanconi anemia patients at single-cell resolution, using the resulting immunophenotypic signatures to stratify leukemic subpopulations by predicted chemosensitivity. Because FA patients cannot tolerate standard-intensity AML induction, treatment must be reduced yet remain effective; CD66 variant profiles offer a functional biomarker layer to identify which blast subclones are most vulnerable to specific agents, enabling rational selection among reduced-intensity options. The single-cell resolution of CyTOF further reveals intra-patient clonal heterogeneity that bulk assays miss, allowing clinicians to anticipate resistance-prone subpopulations and design combination regimens that preemptively target them within the narrow therapeutic window FA patients afford. This convergence transforms FA-adapted AML chemotherapy from empiric dose reduction into a precision oncology strategy anchored in high-dimensional immunophenotypic data.

ideas.title: Single-cell profiling of MSC-treated patients
ideas.description: Use single-cell RNA sequencing to characterize immune cell changes in responders versus non-responders after MSC infusion.
ideas.sourceTitle: Mesenchymal stromal cells for acute graft‐versus‐host disease: response at 1 week predicts probability of survival

ideas.title: AND-gate bispecific targeting of CEACAM1 and BCMA for myeloma-selective cytotoxicity
ideas.description: To overcome the fatal selectivity problem of targeting CEACAM1 alone, this approach employs a bispecific AND-gate construct that activates cytotoxic payload delivery only upon simultaneous engagement of CEACAM1 and the myeloma-enriched antigen BCMA. A split antibody-drug conjugate or conditional T-cell engager architecture ensures that neither antigen alone triggers effector function, confining killing to myeloma plasma cells co-expressing both markers while sparing CEACAM1-positive normal tissues. Preclinical validation would use dual-antigen flow cytometry on patient bone marrow samples to confirm the co-expression window, followed by in vivo efficacy and toxicity studies in BCMA+/CEACAM1+ xenograft models compared to single-target controls.

ideas.title: Biosimilar equivalence may vary by disease subtype
ideas.description: Assuming uniform biosimilar efficacy across all hematological malignancies may mask disease-specific differences in mobilization response.
ideas.sourceTitle: Use of a biosimilar granulocyte colony‐stimulating factor for peripheral blood stem cell mobilization: an analysis of mobilization and engraftment

ideas.title: Biosimilar GCSF in allogeneic donor mobilization
ideas.description: Extending biosimilar GCSF comparison to healthy allogeneic donors would address a distinct clinical population with different risk profiles.
ideas.sourceTitle: Use of a biosimilar granulocyte colony‐stimulating factor for peripheral blood stem cell mobilization: an analysis of mobilization and engraftment

ideas.title: Treating immunosuppressed patients as one group

ideas.title: Adapting HER2-targeted radioimmunotherapy strategies from breast cancer to CD66a/CEACAM1 in myeloma
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from HER2-targeted radioimmunotherapy in breast cancer, where the clinical translation pathway for antibody-radionuclide conjugates has been well established. In that field, trastuzumab was systematically conjugated to diagnostic and therapeutic radionuclides, with biodistribution imaging preceding therapy trials, and bispecific pre-targeting strategies were developed to improve tumor specificity. Adapting this paradigm to CD66a/CEACAM1-targeted myeloma therapy, the proposed approach would begin with imaging studies using indium-111 or zirconium-89 labeled anti-CEACAM1 antibodies to map biodistribution and confirm preferential myeloma uptake in the bone marrow. Upon validation, therapeutic conjugates bearing lutetium-177 or yttrium-90 would enter dose-escalation trials with individualized dosimetry. Because CEACAM1 is expressed on some normal epithelial and immune cells, a pre-targeting strategy using bispecific antibodies (anti-CEACAM1 × anti-hapten) would be employed: the cold bispecific is administered first and allowed to clear from circulation before a small radiolabeled hapten is injected, concentrating radiation at CEACAM1-dense myeloma sites while minimizing collateral damage. This stepwise, imaging-guided, pre-targeting-enhanced framework directly mirrors the successful HER2 radioimmunotherapy development pipeline and addresses the unique challenge of targeting a shared antigen like CEACAM1 in the myeloma setting.

ideas.title: Systematic Genomic Screening for Occult Inherited Syndromes in Adult 'De Novo' AML to Redefine Disease Ontology and Therapeutic Risk
ideas.description: This research proposes a prospective, multi-center study to systematically apply comprehensive germline genetic testing (including Fanconi anemia gene panels and broader inherited bone marrow failure syndrome screens) to all adults diagnosed with apparent 'de novo' AML. The core hypothesis is that a clinically significant subset of these patients harbors occult inherited syndromes, which have been overlooked due to the historical bias that such syndromes only present in childhood. By identifying this cohort, the research aims to redefine a portion of adult AML not as a sporadic disease, but as the late-onset manifestation of a constitutional disorder, fundamentally altering its biological classification. This reclassification carries immediate clinical implications for avoiding DNA-damaging conditioning regimens, informing allogeneic transplant donor selection to avoid affected relatives, and initiating cascade genetic testing for family members.

ideas.title: Organ-specific GvHD response to MSC therapy
ideas.description: Investigate why specific organ involvement influences MSC treatment outcomes to develop organ-targeted therapeutic strategies.

ideas.title: Biomarkers predicting MSC treatment response at infusion
ideas.description: Identify circulating biomarkers at baseline that predict which GvHD patients will respond to MSC therapy within one week.
ideas.sourceTitle: Mesenchymal stromal cells for acute graft‐versus‐host disease: response at 1 week predicts probability of survival

ideas.title: Functional dependency mapping of CEACAM1 across hematologic malignancies: from expression atlas to actionable targets
ideas.description: This study integrates a pan-hematologic malignancy CEACAM1 expression survey (RNA-seq, flow cytometry, and tissue microarray across AML, ALL, CLL, DLBCL, MDS, and myeloma cohorts) with a matched functional dependency screen using CRISPR-Cas9 knockout panels and anti-CEACAM1 blocking antibodies in patient-derived cell lines and xenograft models. By correlating expression levels with proliferation arrest, immune checkpoint modulation, and in vivo tumor control, the pipeline distinguishes disease contexts where CEACAM1 is a functionally essential driver from those where it is a non-functional bystander. Expression-positive but dependency-negative malignancies are flagged to prevent futile therapeutic development, while dependency-validated contexts are prioritized with companion biomarker signatures for clinical trial stratification. This approach transforms a descriptive cataloguing exercise into a mechanistically grounded target prioritization framework with direct translational utility.

ideas.title: TKI may replace rather than adjunct chemotherapy
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that TKI must supplement chemotherapy; TKI-dominant regimens could reduce chemotherapy toxicity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adjuvant tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy improves outcome for children and adolescents with acute lymphoblastic leukaemia who have an ABL‐class fusion

ideas.title: FA/BRCA pathway-deficient AML targeted with CEACAM1-directed radioimmunotherapy
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to first perform comprehensive pan-FA/BRCA pathway screening in AML patient samples to identify those with specific DNA repair deficiencies. For patients harboring these defects, a clinical trial would be initiated using anti-CD66a (CEACAM1) radioimmunotherapy. The core hypothesis is that AML cells with compromised FA/BRCA-mediated DNA repair will be exquisitely sensitive to the DNA double-strand breaks induced by the targeted radiation, leading to enhanced tumor cell kill while sparing healthy tissues. This approach merges precision diagnostics with a targeted cytotoxic modality to create a genotype-directed therapy for a high-risk AML subset.

ideas.title: Patient quality-of-life outcomes with pre-emptive strategy
ideas.description: Formally measure quality-of-life benefits of pre-emptive versus delayed re-mobilization using validated patient-reported outcome instruments.
ideas.sourceTitle: UK consensus statement on the use of plerixafor to facilitate autologous peripheral blood stem cell collection to support high‐dose chemoradiotherapy for patients with malignancy

ideas.title: Oral formulation of a chromosomal fragility-inducing agent for targeted AML cell sensitization and diagnostic enhancement
ideas.description: This research proposes to repurpose and reformulate isoxsuprine—or a similar safe, orally bioavailable agent with mild DNA fragility-inducing properties—into a modified-release oral formulation for human use. The core hypothesis is that administering this agent prior to diagnostic sampling will selectively stress the genome of latent Fanconi Anemia pathway-deficient or other genomically unstable leukemic cells in young adult AML patients, making them more susceptible to induced chromosomal breakage in a subsequent diepoxybutane (DEB) challenge test. This pharmacological 'priming' aims to significantly increase the sensitivity and specificity of the chromosomal breakage assay as a diagnostic tool, while the controlled-release formulation ensures optimal systemic exposure for the sensitization effect. Ultimately, this approach seeks to transform a passive diagnostic test into an active, pharmacologically enhanced procedure that can uncover cryptic therapeutic vulnerabilities.

ideas.title: FA-First Synthetic Lethality Screening Integrated with Constrained Pharmacogenomics
ideas.description: This redesigned approach prioritizes defining the unique drug sensitivity landscape of Fanconi Anemia cells before applying any external pharmacogenomic data. A high-throughput ex vivo platform tests primary FA patient cells against chemotherapy and targeted agent libraries to establish baseline toxicity profiles and identify potential synthetic lethal interactions specific to FA pathway deficiencies. Only agents demonstrating a viable therapeutic index in this FA-centric screen are advanced to a second phase where limited, relevant oncology pharmacogenomic data is used to model individualized dosing and predict resistance mechanisms, strictly within the boundaries of the pre-defined FA safety window. The final output is a patient-specific drug prioritization and dosing guide derived from direct FA biology, making it inherently safer and more robust than a direct translation from standard oncology.

ideas.title: Dual-target CAR-T with CEACAM1 primary recognition and CD138-independent myeloma identification
ideas.description: This fused approach engineers CAR-T cells with anti-CEACAM1 specificity as the primary recognition domain for myeloma plasma cells, directly addressing the critical limitation that CD138 (syndecan-1) is shed from the cell surface during sample processing and potentially during disease progression, which compromises both conventional targeting and treatment monitoring. By leveraging CEACAM1's greater membrane stability on malignant plasma cells, the CAR-T construct achieves more consistent target engagement independent of CD138 shedding dynamics, while a secondary CD138-binding domain can be incorporated as a logic-gated AND-gate to enhance specificity for true plasma cell neoplasms. The design further enables reliable flow cytometric and soluble biomarker tracking of therapeutic response, since CEACAM1 surface density is not confounded by the processing artifacts that plague CD138-based monitoring. This strategy transforms a known limitation of current myeloma diagnostics into a therapeutic advantage, creating a CAR-T platform that is both more targetable and more monitorable than CD138-dependent approaches.

ideas.title: Time-Resolved Multi-Marker Benchmarking Platform for Plasma Cell Identification Beyond CD138
ideas.description: This study establishes a systematic benchmarking framework that evaluates CD138, CD66a, and additional candidate plasma cell markers (CD38, CD319/SLAMF7, VS38c) using split-sample designs where matched aliquots are processed at defined time intervals (0, 1, 2, 4, 8 hours) and analyzed by multi-parameter flow cytometry against paired fresh bone marrow biopsy immunohistochemistry as ground truth. By simultaneously quantifying each marker's degradation kinetics during routine processing and its diagnostic sensitivity/specificity across normal plasma cells, MGUS, smoldering myeloma, and active multiple myeloma samples, the platform generates evidence-based composite scoring rather than assuming any single marker is superior. The output is a validated, processing-time-adjusted marker panel with empirically derived diagnostic thresholds, directly addressing both the shedding artifact problem of CD138 and the unvalidated specificity problem of proposed alternatives like CD66a.

ideas.title: Risk-Stratified FA Pathway Screening in Adult AML Using Integrated Clinical and Genomic Pre-Filters
ideas.description: This study proposes a targeted, cost-effective strategy to identify adult AML patients with underlying Fanconi Anemia by moving from universal screening to a precision-guided approach. A pre-screening model will be developed and validated using clinical features (e.g., personal/family history, physical stigmata), treatment history (prior chemotherapy/radiation), and tumor genomic signatures (e.g., specific somatic mutation patterns associated with FA-related AML) to define a high-risk subgroup. Only patients meeting these pre-defined, high-yield criteria will proceed to definitive FA diagnostic testing, such as chromosomal breakage assays or germline sequencing of FA genes. This system optimizes resource use, increases the positive predictive value of screening, and provides a practical framework for integrating rare germline predisposition testing into adult oncology workflows.

ideas.title: Lipid nanoparticle-based oral delivery of anti-CEACAM1 antibody fragments for hematologic malignancies
ideas.description: This project first conducts a comprehensive CEACAM1 (CD66a) expression survey across all major hematologic malignancies—including leukemias, lymphomas, and myeloproliferative neoplasms—to identify disease contexts where CEACAM1 is consistently overexpressed and functionally relevant. Building on principles from lipid-based and modified-release oral formulation design, the work then develops lipid nanoparticle carriers encapsulating anti-CEACAM1 single-domain antibody fragments (nanobodies) optimized for gastrointestinal absorption and systemic bioavailability. The fusion creates a novel therapeutic paradigm: an oral immunotherapy targeting a validated surface antigen on blood cancers, overcoming the injection-dependent delivery limitation of conventional antibody therapies. Pharmacokinetic and efficacy studies in preclinical hematologic malignancy models will assess whether lipid-formulated oral anti-CEACAM1 nanobodies achieve sufficient plasma exposure to engage the target and suppress tumor growth.

ideas.title: Single-cell multi-omic profiling of CD66 variant heterogeneity linked to microsatellite-defined hereditary predisposition subtypes in myeloid malignancies
ideas.description: This approach applies single-cell RNA sequencing to profile CD66 (CEACAM) family member expression heterogeneity across myeloma and AML patient samples that have been simultaneously genotyped at microsatellite loci associated with Fanconi anemia complementation groups and other hereditary myeloid predisposition syndromes. By overlaying microsatellite-defined germline risk haplotypes onto single-cell transcriptomic clusters, the study can determine whether specific hereditary predisposition backgrounds preferentially expand clones expressing particular CD66 splice variants or paralog combinations. This linkage between germline genetic architecture and intra-tumor surface marker heterogeneity could reveal mechanistic connections between DNA repair pathway deficiencies and aberrant CEACAM regulation, while also providing a combinatorial biomarker framework for distinguishing hereditary from sporadic cases at single-cell resolution. The integrated approach would yield actionable stratification tools that pair genetic risk classification with immunophenotypic heterogeneity mapping for precision therapeutic targeting.

ideas.title: Lessons from cytokeratin vs. vimentin in circulating tumor cell capture: stabilizing marker selection for plasma cell identification
ideas.description: The assumption that CD138 (syndecan-1) is the definitive plasma cell marker mirrors the former reliance on EpCAM as the gold standard for circulating tumor cell (CTC) capture in liquid biopsy oncology. EpCAM proved unreliable because it is shed or downregulated during epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and sample handling, causing systematic undercounting of clinically important CTC subsets. The CTC field resolved this by adopting multi-marker capture panels and marker-agnostic physical enrichment methods, benchmarked against functional and transcriptomic ground truths rather than a single surface protein. Adapting this solution, we propose a study that systematically challenges CD138 supremacy by constructing a composite plasma cell identification panel anchored by CD66a—a marker hypothesized to be more resistant to enzymatic shedding and cryopreservation artifacts. The study would process matched bone marrow samples under varying pre-analytical conditions (immediate staining, 24-hour delay, freeze-thaw) and compare detection sensitivity of CD138-alone, CD66a-alone, and a combined panel (CD138/CD66a/cytoplasmic Ig/CD38) against a functional ground truth of single-cell immunoglobulin secretion assays and scRNA-seq-defined plasma cell transcriptomes. This approach directly imports the CTC field's hard-won lesson: when your gold standard marker is labile, the solution is not to optimize around its fragility but to diversify the identification strategy and redefine the reference standard.

ideas.title: Stochastic Progression Model: FA Genotype as a Modifier of Somatic Evolution Toward AML
ideas.description: This research moves beyond deterministic genotype-to-phenotype prediction by modeling AML risk in Fanconi anemia as a stochastic, multi-step process. The system uses the FA complementation group and mutation to parameterize baseline rates of genomic instability and clonal evolution, which then influence the probability of acquiring necessary somatic leukemogenic events over time. It integrates longitudinal clinical and sequencing data to update individual risk trajectories based on the emergence of pre-malignant clones. The final output is a dynamic, personalized risk curve that quantifies the evolving probability of AML onset, reflecting the inherent randomness of cancer development within a genetically constrained system.

ideas.title: Adapting PD-L1/checkpoint blockade strategies from solid tumors to target CEACAM1-mediated immune evasion in myeloma
ideas.description: This idea draws a direct analogy between CEACAM1 on myeloma plasma cells and PD-L1 on solid tumor cells such as melanoma. In the solid tumor field, the discovery that tumor-expressed PD-L1 engages PD-1 on cytotoxic lymphocytes to create an immunosuppressive microenvironment—while also providing tumor-intrinsic survival advantages—led to the revolutionary development of immune checkpoint inhibitors. CEACAM1 presents a strikingly parallel biology: it is a surface glycoprotein that engages in homophilic (CEACAM1-CEACAM1) and heterophilic (CEACAM1-TIM-3) interactions that inhibit T cell and NK cell function, while its cytoplasmic ITIM domains recruit SHP-1/SHP-2 phosphatases that may promote tumor cell survival signaling through suppression of pro-apoptotic pathways. The adapted research program would: (1) systematically characterize CEACAM1 expression across myeloma disease stages and correlate with immune infiltrate functional status, analogous to early PD-L1 expression profiling in solid tumors; (2) dissect the dual role of CEACAM1 using CRISPR knockout and domain-specific mutants to separately assess immune evasion (extracellular domain interactions with immune cells) versus tumor-intrinsic survival (ITIM-mediated intracellular signaling); (3) develop and test anti-CEACAM1 blocking antibodies or bispecific constructs targeting both CEACAM1 and TIM-3, inspired by the combinatorial checkpoint blockade approaches (anti-PD-1 plus anti-CTLA-4) that proved synergistic in melanoma; and (4) evaluate whether CEACAM1 blockade synergizes with existing myeloma therapies such as daratumumab or CAR-T approaches, mirroring how checkpoint inhibitors were combined with targeted therapies in solid tumors. This cross-disciplinary translation leverages two decades of checkpoint biology knowledge to accelerate understanding of a potentially actionable immune evasion mechanism in myeloma.

ideas.title: Lineage-marker promiscuity: Lessons from neuronal ion channels found in non-excitable cells
ideas.description: The assumption that CD66 expression is restricted to the myeloid lineage mirrors a well-documented pattern in neuroscience and cell physiology: voltage-gated sodium channels were considered definitive markers of excitable cells (neurons and myocytes) until their expression was identified in cancer cells, immune cells, and epithelia. The resolution came through unbiased transcriptomic profiling that revealed widespread low-level expression across tissues, combined with functional studies showing these channels served entirely different biological roles in non-canonical contexts—such as promoting tumor cell migration rather than conducting action potentials. Adapting this approach to the CD66a discovery in plasma cells, researchers should conduct a comprehensive single-cell RNA-seq and proteomics survey of CD66 family members across all hematopoietic lineages, abandoning the binary myeloid-restricted framework in favor of a quantitative expression landscape. Functional studies should then determine whether CD66a in plasma cells serves non-canonical roles distinct from its myeloid functions—potentially in bone marrow niche adhesion, survival signaling, or modulation of immunoglobulin secretion. This analogy predicts that CD66 'lineage restriction' will prove to be a historical artifact of limited detection sensitivity, and that its expression in plasma cells represents a biologically meaningful adaptation rather than aberrant leakage.
