ideas.title: Pharmacokinetic-guided screening for DNA repair deficiencies in young AML using drug metabolism biomarkers
ideas.description: This fused approach proposes developing a pharmacokinetic functional screening platform for young AML patients, where plasma drug concentration profiles after standardized oral dosing of select probe compounds serve as indirect biomarkers of underlying DNA repair pathway deficiencies such as Fanconi anemia. Drawing on the methodology of measuring plasma drug bioavailability to explain variable therapeutic responses, the idea applies this pharmacokinetic phenotyping concept to detect FA-associated metabolic signatures — since FA pathway defects alter aldehyde detoxification and drug metabolism enzymes, patients with occult FA may show distinctive absorption and clearance patterns for specific substrates. This dual screening strategy — pairing rapid pharmacokinetic profiling with confirmatory chromosomal breakage or genetic testing — could enable faster, cheaper triage of the estimated 5-10% of young AML patients harboring undiagnosed FA, allowing earlier treatment modification to avoid the catastrophic toxicity of standard chemotherapy regimens in this vulnerable subgroup.

ideas.title: Receptor isoform heterogeneity mapping: from plant pathogen resistance to myeloma surface antigen diversity
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from plant innate immunity, where assumptions about uniform receptor gene expression across cultivars were overturned by systematic genotype-phenotype correlation studies. Plant biologists found that NBS-LRR resistance gene families harbored cultivar-specific variant expression patterns that explained differential pathogen susceptibility. Adapting this approach, we propose a comprehensive survey of all CD66/CEACAM family members across cytogenetically defined myeloma subgroups. Rather than assuming uniform CD66a expression, we would employ multiplexed flow cytometry and single-cell transcriptomics on a panel of myeloma samples stratified by translocation subtype and copy number alteration status. By mapping CD66 variant expression to genetic background—mirroring how plant geneticists mapped R-gene variants to cultivar lineage—we can identify whether specific myeloma subgroups preferentially express CD66c, CD66e, or other variants. Such findings could reveal new therapeutic targets, refine antibody-based treatment strategies, and explain heterogeneous responses to immunotherapies targeting the CD66 family.

ideas.title: Precision Dosing for FA Chemotherapy via Microbial Ecology Stability Principles
ideas.description: This idea transfers the solution from microbial ecology—where dynamic feedback maintains system stability—to the challenge of individualized chemotherapy for Fanconi Anemia (FA) patients. In bioreactors, engineers prevent collapse by continuously monitoring the microbial community and adjusting inputs. Adapted here, we would apply frequent, multi-parameter biomarker monitoring (clonal dynamics, cytokines, DNA damage response) during treatment for a DNA-repair-deficient FA patient. A computational controller, using principles from ecological stability models, would then dynamically tailor the chemotherapy regimen (drug, dose, schedule) in real-time. This aims to achieve the therapeutic effect of targeting pre-malignant or malignant clones while actively preserving the fragile balance of the patient's compromised bone marrow ecosystem, thereby minimizing toxicity and preventing treatment-induced marrow failure.

ideas.title: Universal Screening for Fanconi Anemia Pathway Defects in Adult AML via a Biomarker Cascade
ideas.description: This idea expands FA diagnostic consideration from pediatric AML to all adult AML patients by adapting a solution from cardiology's approach to screening for familial hypercholesterolemia. The analogous solution used a tiered, cascade screening model, starting with a low-cost, high-sensitivity test (lipid panel) for the entire population, followed by confirmatory genetic testing only for those who screened positive. Adapted to this context, the research proposes implementing universal screening for FA pathway defects in adult AML via a biomarker cascade. All adult AML diagnostic samples would first undergo a rapid, inexpensive screening assay, such as immunohistochemistry for FANCD2 monoubiquitination—a hallmark of an intact FA pathway. Samples showing loss of this marker (screening positive for potential FA deficiency) would then be channeled into the definitive but more costly and labor-intensive diagnostic tests, like chromosomal breakage analysis with diepoxybutane/mitomycin C and genetic sequencing. This adapted approach makes scaling FA screening to the entire adult AML population clinically and economically feasible, potentially identifying a previously overlooked subset of patients with constitutional FA mutations who may benefit from tailored therapies and familial counseling.

ideas.title: Federated Bayesian genotype-phenotype modeling in Fanconi anaemia with functional validation via isogenic iPSC systems
ideas.description: This study establishes a federated analysis platform that pools patient-level data across international Fanconi anaemia registries, harmonizing clinical phenotypes (AML onset, bone marrow failure severity, congenital anomaly spectrum) with deep genotyping including complementation group, specific alleles, and somatic mosaicism status. Bayesian hierarchical models borrow strength across rare variant classes and adjust for polygenic background modifiers and environmental exposures, overcoming the statistical fragility that plagues small-cohort genotype-phenotype studies in ultra-rare diseases. Predicted high-impact genotype-phenotype associations are then functionally validated using CRISPR-engineered isogenic iPSC lines differentiated into hematopoietic progenitors, testing whether specific FA mutations produce measurable differences in DNA repair capacity, proliferative fitness, and transformation susceptibility. This integrated computational-experimental pipeline transforms underpowered observational correlations into mechanistically grounded, clinically actionable risk stratification for FA patients.

ideas.title: Staging biomarker trajectory modeling: Adapting viral load dynamics frameworks to CEACAM1 expression across myeloma progression
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between tracking surface marker expression across myeloma disease stages and the well-established paradigm of longitudinal viral load monitoring in HIV disease progression. In virology, mixed-effects trajectory models and changepoint analyses applied to serial viral load and CD4 measurements revolutionized the ability to predict stage transitions and guide therapeutic intervention timing. Adapting this framework, we propose a longitudinal cohort study collecting serial bone marrow aspirates from patients at MGUS, smoldering myeloma, and active myeloma stages to quantify CEACAM1 expression by multiparameter flow cytometry and single-cell RNA sequencing. Joint longitudinal-survival models would be fitted to link CEACAM1 expression trajectories with time-to-progression events, while Bayesian changepoint detection would identify critical expression thresholds marking impending stage transitions. Individual trajectory slopes and inflection points would serve as dynamic risk-stratification features, potentially identifying smoldering myeloma patients at high risk for near-term progression. This biomarker kinetics approach moves beyond static single-timepoint measurements toward a dynamic monitoring paradigm that has proven transformative in infectious disease management.

ideas.title: Guided warhead delivery to myeloma via CD66a: Lessons from targeted pesticide delivery in agriculture
ideas.description: This idea adapts the targeted delivery paradigm from precision agriculture to oncology. In agricultural science, the challenge of selectively killing pest organisms while sparing beneficial species was solved by conjugating cytotoxic payloads to carrier molecules that exploit species-specific surface transporters—most notably sugar-neonicotinoid conjugates that target aphid-specific sugar uptake mechanisms. The analogous problem in myeloma therapy is delivering cytotoxic agents selectively to malignant plasma cells while minimizing damage to healthy tissues. We propose developing anti-CD66a antibody-drug conjugates for myeloma by applying three cross-domain design principles: First, CD66a (CEACAM1), which is aberrantly overexpressed on myeloma cells, serves as the selective surface target analogous to pest-specific transporters. Second, we employ environmentally responsive cleavable linkers (e.g., valine-citrulline or disulfide-based) that release potent cytotoxins such as MMAE or DM1 only upon receptor-mediated internalization and lysosomal processing, mirroring the pH- and enzyme-dependent release mechanisms optimized in agricultural conjugates. Third, we apply stoichiometric optimization frameworks from agrochemical conjugate research to determine ideal drug-to-antibody ratios that maximize tumor cell killing while maintaining acceptable pharmacokinetic and safety profiles. This cross-disciplinary approach leverages decades of conjugate chemistry optimization from agricultural science to accelerate ADC development against a novel myeloma target, potentially overcoming resistance mechanisms associated with existing therapeutic targets like BCMA and CD38.

ideas.title: Adapting circulating tumor DNA liquid biopsy strategies to CEACAM1-based minimal residual disease detection in myeloma
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from the solid tumor oncology field, where minimal residual disease detection was transformed by circulating tumor DNA liquid biopsy approaches. In colorectal and lung cancers, researchers moved beyond single-marker assays to multi-analyte ctDNA panels combining mutation tracking with methylation signatures, using digital PCR and NGS to achieve sensitivities of 1:100,000. Critically, serial longitudinal sampling with patient-specific quantitative thresholds enabled reliable distinction between true molecular relapse and background noise. Adapting this paradigm to CEACAM1-based MRD detection in myeloma, we propose developing a multi-parameter flow cytometry panel where CD66a/CEACAM1 serves as an anchor marker alongside other aberrant plasma cell antigens (e.g., CD56, CD117, CD81), mirroring the multi-analyte approach of ctDNA panels. Leveraging high-dimensional platforms such as mass cytometry (CyTOF) or spectral flow cytometry would provide the resolution analogous to digital PCR's single-molecule sensitivity. Longitudinal serial sampling at standardized post-treatment intervals, with patient-specific CEACAM1 expression baselines established from pre-treatment and normal bone marrow references, would enable quantitative MRD tracking akin to serial ctDNA monitoring. This approach addresses the key limitation of single-marker MRD assays — insufficient specificity against background — by combining marker multiplexing with individualized longitudinal thresholds, potentially achieving MRD sensitivity beyond the current 10^-5 standard in myeloma.

ideas.title: Functional genomic phenotyping integrated with targeted sequencing for hereditary AML predisposition classification
ideas.description: This approach abandons indirect linkage markers in favor of directly assaying the cellular phenotypes and genetic lesions characteristic of different AML predisposition syndromes. Patient-derived cells are subjected to a standardized panel of functional assays measuring DNA damage response, chromosomal breakage, and pathway activation to generate a mechanistic phenotypic profile. This functional profile is then integrated with targeted deep sequencing of a comprehensive gene panel covering known and candidate predisposition genes. The combined functional and mutational data provides a direct, individualized classification of the underlying predisposition syndrome, enabling precise risk stratification and therapeutic guidance without reliance on familial linkage data.

ideas.title: Mapping normal-state receptor function before malignant transformation: Lessons from precursor astrocyte glutamate transporter studies in glioma
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from neuro-oncology, where understanding the normal function of the glutamate transporter EAAT2 in healthy astrocytes proved essential for deciphering its role in glioblastoma pathogenesis. Researchers first built a comprehensive functional baseline of EAAT2 in non-malignant astrocytes—mapping expression, splice variants, protein interactions, and signaling consequences—then systematically compared this against tumor cells to identify transformation-associated changes. Adapting this approach to CD66a (CEACAM1) on bone marrow plasma cells, the proposed research would: (1) perform single-cell transcriptomic and proteomic profiling of CD66a on normal bone marrow plasma cells to establish expression baselines, isoform ratios (particularly the balance between the inhibitory long-tail CEACAM1-L and the activating short-tail CEACAM1-S), and co-expression networks; (2) use functional assays (adhesion, immune checkpoint engagement via CEACAM1-CEACAM1 homophilic and CEACAM1-TIM3 heterophilic interactions, ITIM/ITSM phosphatase recruitment) to define the normal signaling repertoire; (3) create a staged comparison across the myeloma progression spectrum (normal plasma cells → MGUS → smoldering myeloma → multiple myeloma) to identify precisely when and how CD66a function diverges from the healthy baseline. This normal-first strategy, borrowed from the astrocyte-glioma paradigm, avoids the common pitfall of studying a molecule only in its disease context, enabling identification of whether CD66a loss, overexpression, or isoform switching is a driver versus passenger event in myelomagenesis.

ideas.title: Adapting Non-Destructive Testing for AML: A Chromosomal Stress Assay Panel
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of non-destructive proof testing from materials engineering to AML diagnostics. In materials science, components are subjected to a controlled stress to reveal hidden flaws. Analogously, we propose applying a panel of low-dose, mechanistically diverse genotoxic stresses (e.g., etoposide, radiation, a PARP inhibitor) to cells from young adult AML patients, rather than relying solely on the Fanconi anemia-specific diepoxybutane test. The resulting chromosomal breakage 'fingerprint' across this stress panel would serve as a functional assay to diagnose specific subtypes of genomic instability, stratify patients based on underlying repair deficiencies, and potentially predict sensitivity to targeted therapies like PARP inhibitors or ATR inhibitors, thus transferring and expanding the methodological concept of stress-induced breakage testing.

ideas.title: Adapting single-cell spatial clonal decomposition from tumor ecology to resolve CD66 variant heterogeneity in myeloma
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from microbial ecology, where the field transitioned from bulk amplicon sequencing to single-cell metagenomics and spatial metatranscriptomics to resolve strain-level heterogeneity within complex biofilm communities. In that domain, droplet-based single-cell genome sequencing and FISH-guided spatial methods enabled researchers to identify rare bacterial variants with distinct functional roles (e.g., antibiotic resistance) that were invisible in bulk measurements. Adapting this framework to myeloma, we propose using CITE-seq (Cellular Indexing of Transcriptomes and Epitopes by Sequencing) to simultaneously profile CD66/CEACAM family surface protein isoforms and transcriptomic states at single-cell resolution from myeloma patient bone marrow aspirates. This would replace bulk flow cytometry, which collapses heterogeneous CD66 expression into population averages, with a method capable of resolving distinct myeloma subclones bearing different CD66 splice variants or expression levels. Additionally, integrating spatial transcriptomics (e.g., 10x Visium or MERFISH) would map the spatial distribution of CD66-variant subclones relative to the bone marrow niche architecture—analogous to how spatial metatranscriptomics revealed the functional geography of biofilms. The expected outcome is identification of CD66-high subclones associated with immune evasion or drug resistance, enabling more precise therapeutic targeting and patient stratification in multiple myeloma.

ideas.title: Precision-guided CAR-T for myeloma inspired by agricultural biocontrol target switching
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from agricultural biocontrol, where microbial agents engineered to kill specific plant pathogens were successfully repurposed for entirely different crop-pathogen systems by modifying their target-recognition domains while preserving core effector mechanisms. In the analogous scenario, Pseudomonas fluorescens strains carrying engineered surface adhesins for binding Fusarium on wheat were retargeted to Botrytis on grape by swapping binding domains—demonstrating that a proven biological killing platform can be redirected across ecological niches with targeted molecular modifications. Adapting this principle, we propose retargeting anti-CEACAM1 CAR-T cells—originally validated against CEACAM1-expressing solid tumors such as melanoma and colorectal cancer—to multiple myeloma. CEACAM1 is expressed on malignant plasma cells and contributes to immune evasion in the bone marrow niche. The key adaptation, inspired by the biocontrol analogy, is that simply swapping the target is insufficient; the effector platform must be tuned to the new microenvironment. Specifically, the CAR construct should retain the anti-CEACAM1 scFv but incorporate modified hinge/spacer regions and costimulatory domains (e.g., substituting CD28 for 4-1BB or adding ICOS signaling) optimized for the immunosuppressive bone marrow milieu, which differs fundamentally from the solid tumor stroma. Additionally, armored CAR-T variants secreting IL-15 or expressing dominant-negative TGF-beta receptors could counteract myeloma-associated immune suppression, paralleling how biocontrol strains were engineered with additional antibiotic or siderophore genes to thrive in new ecological contexts. This cross-domain transfer leverages both the validated target (CEACAM1) and the proven principle that biological effector systems can be successfully redirected across niches through systematic domain engineering.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Clustering of AML Predisposition Genes Using Linkage-Informed Markers
ideas.description: This idea adapts a phylogenetic classification method from evolutionary biology to the problem of subtyping hereditary Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) predisposition syndromes. In phylogenetics, conserved genetic markers are used to determine evolutionary relationships and classify species. The analogous solution is adapted by treating various AML predisposition gene pathways as analogous to evolutionary clades. A defined panel of microsatellite markers, whose linkage disequilibrium and haplotype patterns have been established as fingerprints for specific genetic pathways (initially through Fanconi Anemia complementation group analysis), will be used as the conserved 'markers'. DNA from patients with known and unknown hereditary AML syndromes will be genotyped for this panel. Phylogenetic clustering algorithms (e.g., neighbor-joining, maximum parsimony) will then be applied to the resulting haplotype data to generate a disease classification tree. This method will provide an objective, linkage-based system to categorize new or atypical AML predisposition cases into defined molecular subtypes, potentially revealing novel relationships between different gene defects.

ideas.title: Applying Population Genetics Screening to Unmask Cryptic Germline Predisposition in Adult AML
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that adult AML diagnosed without a family history is purely de novo. Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology, where genomic scans reveal hidden signatures of natural selection in apparent neutral variation, we propose adapting population genetics methodologies to AML genomics. The analogous solution involves analyzing patterns of genetic diversity and allele frequencies to distinguish evolutionary events. Adapted to AML, this means developing a bioinformatic framework to scan the somatic mutation data of 'de novo' AML cases for patterns (e.g., specific mutational signatures, constrained clonal architecture, pathway enrichments) that serve as footprints of a vulnerable genomic background, thereby unmasking cryptic inherited bone marrow failure syndromes like Fanconi anaemia that predisposed the patient to leukemia.

ideas.title: Predicting AML Risk Trajectories in FA: A Seismic Hazard Model Approach
ideas.description: This research adapts the core methodology of probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA) from geophysics to the inverse problem of predicting AML onset from FA genotype. In PSHA, the type of geological fault and its slip rate are used to model the probability and timing of future earthquakes. Analogously, we treat each FA complementation group and specific mutation as a distinct 'genetic fault system' with an inherent rate of accumulating the genomic damage necessary for leukemogenesis. By integrating existing clinical registry data on AML onset ages for different genotypes into time-to-event statistical models (akin to seismic renewal models), the solution generates personalized AML hazard curves. These curves would quantitatively predict both the probability and likely timing of AML development over a patient's lifespan, scaling from population-level genotype risks to individual prognostic estimates.

ideas.title: Variant-resolved phenotype mapping in Fanconi anaemia via cystic fibrosis theratype frameworks
ideas.description: This research adapts the highly successful genotype-phenotype classification framework from cystic fibrosis to Fanconi anaemia. In CF, over 2,000 CFTR variants were organized into six functional classes reflecting distinct molecular defects (protein synthesis failure, misfolding, gating dysfunction, reduced conductance, reduced abundance, and destabilized protein), enabling clinicians to predict disease trajectory and select mutation-specific therapies. Analogously, FAA pathway genes can be stratified into mechanistic classes: (1) core complex assembly defects (e.g., truncating FANCA mutations abolishing monoubiquitination), (2) partial core complex dysfunction (e.g., missense FANCA/FANCG variants with residual activity), (3) ID2 complex or ubiquitination-specific defects, and (4) downstream effector failures (e.g., BRCA2/FANCD1 mutations impairing homologous recombination). By aggregating multi-center patient registries and systematically mapping each mechanistic class to quantitative clinical endpoints—median age of myelodysplasia/AML onset, hematologic response to androgens, severity of VACTERL-type anomalies, and solid tumor incidence—this framework would enable phenotype prediction from genotype. The approach would use survival analysis, latent class modeling, and functional validation of variant pathogenicity in isogenic cell lines with FA pathway reconstitution assays. Ultimately, this mechanistic classification could support risk-stratified bone marrow transplant timing, targeted surveillance schedules for mutation-specific cancer predispositions, and rational selection of emerging therapies such as gene therapy or pathway-specific small molecules.

ideas.title: FA/BRCA Pathway Network Analysis via Ecological Succession Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from community ecology—specifically the modeling of whole ecosystems and their response to perturbation—to scale FA/BRCA pathway analysis in AML. The analogous problem in ecology is scaling from studying a single keystone species to modeling entire community networks to understand ecosystem resilience. The adapted research idea involves treating the FA/BRCA DNA repair pathway as a functional biological network or 'ecosystem.' By mapping genetic interactions and modeling the pathway's stability under various mutational 'perturbations' across all complementation groups and associated genes (like BRCA1/2), we can move from single-gene analysis to a pan-pathway screening approach. This will identify network properties that predict leukemogenic collapse, define functional modules ('guilds'), and reveal therapeutic vulnerabilities based on network resilience, rather than isolated gene defects.
